Block 3.7 · deep MCQ review

Understand the mechanism.
Then choose the answer.

Work through each question like a pathology problem: begin with normal physiology, trace the molecular or cellular lesion, follow the pathophysiology into morphology, laboratory findings and clinical effects, then compare every option. Prof. Edmund Muonir Der's Block 3.7 slides remain the primary exam authority; Pathology Secrets is used to deepen genuine gaps.

258MCQs
34flagged items
6exam sections
Important exam correction: Prof. Der's current slide uses >20% blasts in blood or marrow as the general acute-leukaemia threshold, with defined genetic exceptions. Older past questions that force obsolete cut-offs are marked rather than silently memorised.

2025

62 questions
You
2025 · Question 1
Confirmed
Which of the following will increase the risk of Hodgkin's lymphoma
AI
Answer: D — Infective mononucleosis
Confirmed · Hodgkin lymphoma, EBV risk & classic clinical associations

1. Start with the normal physiology

Germinal-center B cells that fail appropriate selection normally undergo apoptosis. Immune-cell interactions within lymph nodes are tightly regulated by cytokine networks.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Hodgkin lymphoma contains a minority of neoplastic Reed–Sternberg cells within a prominent reactive inflammatory background. In a subset, EBV contributes to oncogenic signalling and survival; prior infectious mononucleosis therefore increases later Hodgkin-lymphoma risk. Clinical disease often presents with painless lymphadenopathy and constitutional ‘B symptoms’; Pel–Ebstein fever is a classic historical association.

Susceptible B cell ± EBV-driven survival signalling → Reed–Sternberg clone → cytokine-rich inflammatory microenvironment → lymphadenopathy/B symptoms

3. Why this answer follows

Infectious mononucleosis is the clinical syndrome of primary EBV infection. EBV establishes latency in B cells; in susceptible cells viral latent proteins can provide survival and proliferative signals. Hodgkin lymphoma is heterogeneous, but EBV genomes/proteins are found in a meaningful subset, so a history of infectious mononucleosis is a recognized epidemiologic risk association—not because mononucleosis itself transforms every B cell, but because it marks a biologic exposure capable of contributing to later lymphomagenesis.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Hypothyroidism why not / analysis
Hypothyroidism is an endocrine state and is not the classic epidemiologic risk association being tested for Hodgkin lymphoma.
B. Alcoholism why not / analysis
Alcohol use can affect nutrition, liver function and marrow physiology, but it is not the specific Hodgkin-lymphoma risk association in this stem.
C. Pregnancy why not / analysis
Pregnancy changes immune and haemostatic physiology but is not the established risk association targeted by this question.
D. Infective mononucleosis correct
Correct. This reflects primary EBV infection, the relevant lymphoma association.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 EBV/infectious mononucleosis is an association, not a statement that every Hodgkin lymphoma is EBV-positive.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 356–360. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 2
Confirmed
The serum of an individual with blood group B contains
AI
Answer: D — Anti A antibodies
Confirmed · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Group B RBCs express B carbohydrate antigen. During early life, exposure to environmental antigens resembling A/B structures leads to naturally occurring ABO isohaemagglutinins against the antigen a person lacks. Therefore a group B person lacks A antigen and has anti-A in plasma. Anti-B would attack the person's own B-positive RBCs and is therefore not normally present.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies why not / analysis
Both antibodies are characteristic of group O plasma, not group B plasma.
B. Anti D antibodies why not / analysis
Anti-D is not a naturally occurring ABO isohaemagglutinin; it usually develops after immune exposure to the D antigen.
C. Anti B antibodies why not / analysis
A group B person expresses B antigen and therefore should not normally have anti-B; that would be autoreactive.
D. Anti A antibodies correct
Correct. A group B person lacks A antigen and therefore normally has anti-A in plasma.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.
Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 3
Confirmed
Which of the following are characteristics of acute myeloid leukemia .
AI
Answer: D — All of the above
Confirmed · Acute leukaemia: clonal biology, marrow failure & blast thresholds

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal haematopoiesis requires progenitor proliferation to remain coupled to orderly differentiation and apoptosis. Mature cells leave marrow while immature blasts remain a small minority.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Acute leukaemia arises when acquired genetic lesions give a clone increased proliferation/survival and—critically—a block in differentiation. Blasts accumulate in marrow, replacing normal haematopoiesis. Anaemia causes fatigue, neutropenia causes infection, thrombocytopenia causes bleeding, and high circulating blast counts can cause leukostasis. Prof. Der’s current slides use >20% blasts in blood or marrow as the general diagnostic threshold, with genetically defined exceptions; the older FAB system used 30%.

Driver mutations → ↑proliferation + ↓apoptosis + maturation arrest → blast accumulation → marrow replacement → cytopenias ± leukostasis/infiltration

3. Why this answer follows

AML is a clonal myeloid precursor malignancy with maturation arrest. Blasts crowd the marrow and suppress normal erythropoiesis, granulopoiesis and megakaryopoiesis, causing anaemia, infection risk and bleeding. Blasts may spill into blood and the total WBC may be high, normal or low; thus leukocytosis can occur, but the biologic hallmark is blast accumulation plus marrow failure.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Increased amounts of leucocytic blast in the bone marrow with bleeding tendencies. why not / analysis
Bone itself is not the classic target; bone marrow is affected because haematopoietic precursors divide rapidly.
B. Immature leukocytes and blast cells in peripheral blood and anemia why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
C. Leukocytosis why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
D. All of the above correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A high total WBC does not mean effective immunity: the cells may be malignant blasts. Also watch for old 30% FAB-threshold questions that conflict with current >20% criteria.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 260–275. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 4
Confirmed
Which of the following is an immune-related cause of neutropenia?
AI
Answer: E — Felty's syndrome
Confirmed · Leukocyte physiology, lymphocyte biology & reactive counts

1. Start with the normal physiology

Neutrophils arise in marrow through myeloblast → promyelocyte → myelocyte → metamyelocyte → band → segmented stages, with a large marrow storage pool allowing rapid release. B cells mature in marrow, T cells in thymus, and antigen-driven immune responses occur in secondary lymphoid tissues such as lymph nodes, spleen and mucosal lymphoid tissue. Mature helper T cells are typically CD4-positive/CD8-negative and coordinate immune responses through cytokines.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Leukocyte counts change through altered production, release, margination, tissue migration or destruction. Reactive lymphocytosis accompanies selected infections such as pertussis, infectious mononucleosis, measles and hepatitis. Immune neutropenia can occur when antibodies or immune dysregulation increase peripheral neutrophil destruction, as in Felty syndrome.

Stimulus/disease → altered marrow production or peripheral distribution/destruction → characteristic leukocyte-count pattern

3. Why this answer follows

Felty syndrome classically combines rheumatoid arthritis, splenomegaly and neutropenia. Immune-mediated peripheral destruction/sequestration and altered granulopoiesis contribute. By contrast, chronic granulomatous disease and myeloperoxidase deficiency are mainly defects of neutrophil killing, while Chediak–Higashi and lazy-leukocyte syndromes are functional/trafficking disorders rather than the classic immune neutropenia asked here.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Chediak-Higashi syndrome why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. Lazy leukocyte syndrome why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. Chronic granulomatous disease of childhood why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. Myeloperoxidase deficiency why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
E. Felty's syndrome correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Differentiate a quantitative disorder (too many/too few cells) from a functional disorder (cells present but defective). Several distractors are neutrophil-function syndromes, not causes of neutropenia.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 186–190. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 5
Confirmed + note
The purpose of a major cross-match in transfusion medicine is to?
AI
Answer: E — Detect recipient antibodies/incompatibility against donor red-cell antigens — thereby ensuring compatible, safe blood.
Confirmed + note · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

The major crossmatch mixes recipient plasma/serum with donor RBCs. This directly asks: ‘Does the recipient have an antibody capable of binding and destroying these donor red cells?’ Detecting agglutination or antiglobulin reactivity prevents an immune haemolytic transfusion reaction. Blood grouping is necessary but does not replace this compatibility check for unexpected alloantibodies.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. To identify the donor blood group why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. To identify the patient's blood group why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
C. To give safe blood why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
E. To detect atypical antibodies that are directed against foreign antigens on donor RBCs correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

Choose the option that states detection of atypical/recipient antibodies against donor RBC antigens. In versions where that option is garbled, that is the intended concept.

Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 6
Confirmed
In transfusion medicine, forward blood grouping involves?
AI
Answer: A — Patient RBCs are added to test tubes that contain either anti-A or anti-B test serum.
Confirmed · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Forward typing identifies antigens present on the patient's RBC membrane by exposing the cells to known anti-A and anti-B reagents. Agglutination with anti-A means A antigen is present; agglutination with anti-B means B antigen is present. Reverse typing does the opposite: it tests patient plasma against known A and B reagent cells.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Patient RBCs are added to test tubes that contain either anti-A or anti-B test serum. correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. Patient RBCs are added to test tubes that contain exogenous antigens why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
C. No agglutination reaction why not / analysis
This is an antiplatelet/vasodilator product of intact endothelium.
D. Patient serum is added to test tubes containing either A or B test RBCs why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
E. Agglutination at room temperature why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.
Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You

Confirmed + note
The purpose of a major cross match in transfusion medicine is to?
AI
Answer: D — Detect recipient antibodies/incompatibility against donor red-cell antigens — thereby ensuring compatible, safe blood.
Confirmed + note · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

The major crossmatch mixes recipient plasma/serum with donor RBCs. This directly asks: ‘Does the recipient have an antibody capable of binding and destroying these donor red cells?’ Detecting agglutination or antiglobulin reactivity prevents an immune haemolytic transfusion reaction. Blood grouping is necessary but does not replace this compatibility check for unexpected alloantibodies.

A. To identify the donor's blood group why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

C. To give safe blood why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. To detect atypical antibodies that are directed against foreign antigens on donor RBCs correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
E. To prevent haemolytic disease of the new born why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

2025 · Question 8
Confirmed
Which one of the following organs is commonly affected in vitamin B12 deficiency?
AI
Answer: B — The mucosa on the gastrointestinal system
Confirmed · Vitamin B12 physiology & megaloblastic pathology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Food-bound cobalamin is released in the stomach by acid and pepsin, initially binds salivary/gastric R-binders (cobalophilins/haptocorrin), is freed by pancreatic proteases in the duodenum, then binds intrinsic factor from gastric parietal cells. The intrinsic-factor–B12 complex is absorbed in the terminal ileum and B12 circulates bound mainly to transcobalamin II.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

B12 deficiency impairs methionine synthase activity and traps folate as methyl-THF, producing functionally inadequate tetrahydrofolate for DNA synthesis. Rapidly dividing tissues develop nuclear–cytoplasmic asynchrony: cytoplasm matures while nuclear DNA replication lags. This causes megaloblastic marrow change, macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils and ineffective intramedullary haematopoiesis. B12 deficiency also causes neurologic injury through disordered myelin metabolism—an important distinction from isolated folate deficiency.

Impaired B12 availability → defective DNA synthesis + abnormal myelin metabolism → megaloblastosis/ineffective erythropoiesis + neurologic disease

3. Why this answer follows

B12 deficiency injures rapidly dividing tissues because defective DNA synthesis is most visible where cell turnover is high. The GI mucosa develops epithelial megaloblastosis/atrophy and glossitis; the bone marrow becomes megaloblastic; neurologic tissue is also uniquely affected through myelin injury. In the wording of this slide-based MCQ, GI mucosa is the intended organ/tissue answer.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Brain why not / analysis
B12 deficiency can cause important neurologic injury, but the slide-based organ/tissue wording here targets rapidly proliferating GI mucosa.
B. The mucosa on the gastrointestinal system correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. Bone why not / analysis
Bone itself is not the classic target; bone marrow is affected because haematopoietic precursors divide rapidly.
D. Kidney why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.
E. Liver why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Follow B12 through the GI tract step by step. A question may target the first binder, intrinsic factor source, terminal ileal absorption, pernicious anaemia or neurologic consequences.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 81–84. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 9
Confirmed
Neural tube is associated with which nutritional anaemia
AI
Answer: D — folate
Confirmed · Folate one-carbon metabolism & neural-tube biology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Folate is converted to tetrahydrofolate derivatives that shuttle one-carbon units needed for purine synthesis and conversion of deoxyuridylate to thymidylate. This is crucial for DNA replication in rapidly proliferating tissues. Body stores are limited compared with B12, so deficiency can develop relatively quickly when intake is poor or requirements rise.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Deficiency slows DNA synthesis, producing megaloblastic change in marrow and mucosa. During very early embryogenesis, rapid proliferation is required for neural-tube closure; inadequate folate availability increases the risk of neural-tube defects. Folate can correct the haematologic abnormality of B12 deficiency while allowing neurologic injury to progress, which is why B12 deficiency must be excluded before treating an unexplained megaloblastic anaemia with folate alone.

↓Folate/THF one-carbon transfer → ↓thymidylate/purines → defective DNA synthesis → megaloblastosis; in embryo → impaired neural-tube closure

3. Why this answer follows

Neural-tube closure occurs very early in embryogenesis and requires intense DNA synthesis and cell proliferation. Folate-derived one-carbon units are required for thymidylate and purine synthesis. Deficiency therefore impairs proliferative closure of the neural folds, increasing risk of spina bifida, anencephaly and related defects. This is why folate supplementation must begin before conception or very early in pregnancy.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. iron why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. vitamin B12 why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. pernicious why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. folate correct
Correct. Folate is an essential nutritional factor for DNA synthesis; in Prof. Der's slide taxonomy it is listed separately from the ‘cofactor’ group.
E. vitamin B6 why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Link “folate” to DNA synthesis, not just to a memorized disease name. That mechanism explains marrow, GI and fetal manifestations.
Source basis: Block 3.7, p. 87. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 10
Confirmed
The concentration of HbA2 in a neonate is?
AI
Answer: C — 0.5%
Confirmed · Haemoglobin structure, switching & haemoglobinopathies

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemoglobin is a tetramer of globin chains, each carrying haem. Developmental globin switching matches oxygen affinity to fetal and postnatal physiology: embryonic haemoglobins appear early, HbF (α2γ2) dominates fetal life, and HbA (α2β2) becomes dominant after birth; HbA2 (α2δ2) remains a small adult fraction.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Qualitative haemoglobinopathies change globin structure (for example HbS or HbC), whereas thalassaemias reduce synthesis of a globin chain, creating chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Severe β-thalassaemia causes profound marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure and increased HbF because β-chain production is absent or markedly reduced.

Globin gene abnormality → abnormal structure OR reduced chain synthesis → unstable/imbalanced Hb → ineffective erythropoiesis ± haemolysis → anaemia and systemic complications

3. Why this answer follows

HbA2 is α2δ2. Delta-chain synthesis is minimal at birth, so neonatal HbA2 is very low, while HbF (α2γ2) remains dominant. After birth γ-chain synthesis falls and β-chain synthesis rises, increasing HbA; HbA2 gradually reaches its small adult fraction.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 24% why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. 2.5℅ why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. 0.5% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. 97% why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
E. 12% why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Always ask whether the defect is qualitative (structure) or quantitative (amount). Hb electrophoresis patterns then become logical rather than rote.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 11
Confirmed
Which of the following transport proteins is synthesized in the stomach?
AI
Answer: B — Intrinsic factor
Confirmed · Vitamin B12 physiology & megaloblastic pathology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Food-bound cobalamin is released in the stomach by acid and pepsin, initially binds salivary/gastric R-binders (cobalophilins/haptocorrin), is freed by pancreatic proteases in the duodenum, then binds intrinsic factor from gastric parietal cells. The intrinsic-factor–B12 complex is absorbed in the terminal ileum and B12 circulates bound mainly to transcobalamin II.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

B12 deficiency impairs methionine synthase activity and traps folate as methyl-THF, producing functionally inadequate tetrahydrofolate for DNA synthesis. Rapidly dividing tissues develop nuclear–cytoplasmic asynchrony: cytoplasm matures while nuclear DNA replication lags. This causes megaloblastic marrow change, macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils and ineffective intramedullary haematopoiesis. B12 deficiency also causes neurologic injury through disordered myelin metabolism—an important distinction from isolated folate deficiency.

Impaired B12 availability → defective DNA synthesis + abnormal myelin metabolism → megaloblastosis/ineffective erythropoiesis + neurologic disease

3. Why this answer follows

Intrinsic factor is a glycoprotein secreted by gastric parietal cells. It is not the first molecule that binds dietary B12—that role belongs to haptocorrin/cobalophilin—but it is the indispensable gastric protein that later binds B12 in the duodenum and permits receptor-mediated absorption in the terminal ileum.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Transcobalamines why not / analysis
Transcobalamin transports B12 in plasma after intestinal uptake; it is not the first gastric binder and is not intrinsic factor.
B. Intrinsic factor correct
Correct. Intrinsic factor is made by gastric parietal cells and is required for terminal-ileal B12 uptake, but it is not the first binder of food-released B12.
C. Cobalamines why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. Cobalophilines why not / analysis
Cobalophilin/haptocorrin (R-binder) is the first carrier that binds food-released B12 in the upper GI tract.
E. Globulins why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Follow B12 through the GI tract step by step. A question may target the first binder, intrinsic factor source, terminal ileal absorption, pernicious anaemia or neurologic consequences.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 12
Confirmed
What percentage of oral iron is absorbed in the gastrointestinal tract?
AI
Answer: A — 5-10%
Confirmed · Iron physiology, absorption & iron-deficiency anaemia

1. Start with the normal physiology

Iron is essential for haem synthesis. Dietary non-haem iron is best absorbed in the duodenum/proximal small bowel after reduction to the ferrous form; haem iron is absorbed more efficiently. Enterocyte iron can be stored in ferritin or exported through ferroportin to plasma transferrin. Hepcidin from the liver is the master negative regulator: it binds ferroportin and promotes its internalization, reducing intestinal absorption and macrophage iron release.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Iron deficiency first depletes stores (falling ferritin), then reduces circulating available iron and transferrin saturation, and finally limits haemoglobin synthesis. Erythroblasts undergo extra divisions while trying to reach an adequate intracellular haemoglobin concentration, producing microcytic, hypochromic cells. Chronic blood loss—especially gastrointestinal blood loss in older adults—is a classic mechanism.

↓Iron stores → ↓haem synthesis → delayed haemoglobinization → extra erythroblast divisions → microcytosis + hypochromia → reduced O₂ carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

Only a minority of ingested iron is absorbed under ordinary conditions because intestinal uptake is tightly regulated to match body needs and there is no physiologic pathway for active iron excretion. Prof. Der's slide gives about 5–10% overall absorption; absorption rises when iron stores are low and hepcidin falls.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 5-10% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. 1-3% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. 35-40% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. 15-25% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
E. 0.5% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Know both the anatomy (duodenum) and the regulatory physiology (hepcidin–ferroportin). “Iron absorbed where?” and “why microcytic?” are two faces of the same pathway.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 71–74. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 13
Confirmed
The first organ of haematopoiesis in a foetus is?
AI
Answer: C — York sac
Confirmed · Haematopoiesis & marrow physiology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal blood-cell production begins with a rare self-renewing CD34-positive pluripotent haematopoietic stem cell in the marrow. Stem cells balance self-renewal with commitment into myeloid and lymphoid progenitors. The marrow stromal microenvironment, adhesion molecules, cytokines and lineage-specific growth factors regulate survival, proliferation and differentiation. In fetal life the dominant sites shift from yolk sac to liver/spleen and finally bone marrow; in adults active marrow is concentrated in the axial skeleton and proximal long bones.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Disease appears when one of four things goes wrong: the stem/progenitor compartment is quantitatively lost, maturation is blocked, proliferation becomes autonomous, or mature cells are destroyed faster than the marrow can replace them. The peripheral blood count is therefore the end result of marrow production, release, distribution, sequestration and destruction—not simply a measure of how many cells the marrow is making.

Stem cell → committed progenitor → growth-factor signalling → maturation → marrow release → peripheral survival/distribution

3. Why this answer follows

Primitive haematopoiesis begins in blood islands of the yolk sac. As embryonic development proceeds, the liver becomes the major site, the spleen contributes, and bone marrow progressively becomes dominant. The question is therefore about the earliest site, not the eventual principal fetal site.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Bone marrow why not / analysis
Bone itself is not the classic target; bone marrow is affected because haematopoietic precursors divide rapidly.
B. Thymus why not / analysis
The thymus is a primary lymphoid organ where T cells mature and undergo selection.
C. York sac correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Kidney why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.
E. Skull why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Prof. Der often asks the sequence, site, growth factor or developmental stage rather than naming the process directly. Always locate the question on this production pathway.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 3–17. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 14
Confirmed
The hormone that acts as a sensor in haematopoiesis in humans is?
AI
Answer: C — Erythropoietin
Confirmed · Erythropoiesis, oxygen sensing & red-cell indices

1. Start with the normal physiology

Red-cell production is regulated primarily by tissue oxygen delivery. Renal peritubular interstitial cells sense hypoxia through oxygen-dependent HIF signalling; hypoxia stabilizes HIF, increasing erythropoietin (EPO). EPO supports survival and proliferation of late erythroid progenitors, increasing reticulocyte output. A mature RBC is a deformable biconcave disc packed with haemoglobin and normally survives about 120 days.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Anaemia lowers oxygen delivery, so a healthy marrow should increase reticulocyte production. A high corrected reticulocyte count therefore points toward peripheral loss/destruction with preserved marrow reserve; a low corrected count points toward impaired production. Morphologic indices answer a different question: MCV classifies cell size, MCH/MCHC reflect haemoglobinization, and the haematocrit estimates packed red-cell volume.

↓O₂ delivery → renal hypoxia sensing/HIF → ↑EPO → erythroid progenitor survival → ↑reticulocytes → restoration of oxygen-carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

Strictly, oxygen tension is what is sensed and EPO is the hormonal output of that sensing system. In Prof. Der's teaching language, erythropoietin functions as the key oxygen-responsive hormonal regulator: tissue hypoxia increases renal EPO, which expands late erythroid progenitors. Thus the intended MCQ answer is EPO.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Oxygen why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. Haemoglobin why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. Erythropoietin correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Severe anaemia why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
E. Thrombophylli why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Do not interpret a raw reticulocyte percentage in anaemia without correcting it for the reduced red-cell mass. Separate “production response” from “cell morphology.”
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 3–17. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2025 · Question 15
Confirmed
In a 34 yr old healthy adult, what is the rate of haemopoiesis?
AI
Answer: B — 1013 new cells replaced daily
Confirmed · Haematopoiesis & marrow physiology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal blood-cell production begins with a rare self-renewing CD34-positive pluripotent haematopoietic stem cell in the marrow. Stem cells balance self-renewal with commitment into myeloid and lymphoid progenitors. The marrow stromal microenvironment, adhesion molecules, cytokines and lineage-specific growth factors regulate survival, proliferation and differentiation. In fetal life the dominant sites shift from yolk sac to liver/spleen and finally bone marrow; in adults active marrow is concentrated in the axial skeleton and proximal long bones.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Disease appears when one of four things goes wrong: the stem/progenitor compartment is quantitatively lost, maturation is blocked, proliferation becomes autonomous, or mature cells are destroyed faster than the marrow can replace them. The peripheral blood count is therefore the end result of marrow production, release, distribution, sequestration and destruction—not simply a measure of how many cells the marrow is making.

Stem cell → committed progenitor → growth-factor signalling → maturation → marrow release → peripheral survival/distribution

3. Why this answer follows

Blood-cell turnover is enormous because mature cells have finite life spans: granulocytes may survive hours to days, platelets about a week, and RBCs about 120 days. To maintain steady-state counts, the marrow produces on the order of 10^13 haematopoietic cells daily, illustrating why marrow disorders rapidly become clinically apparent.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 1011 new cells replaced daily why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. 1013 new cells replaced daily correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. 1012 new cells replaced daily why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. 1010 new cells replaced daily why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
E. 1014 new cells replaced daily why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Prof. Der often asks the sequence, site, growth factor or developmental stage rather than naming the process directly. Always locate the question on this production pathway.

You
2025 · Question 16
Confirmed
The commonest haemoglobin found in cord blood of a baby born with Down syndrome is?
AI
Answer: C — 2alpha/2gamma globin chains
Confirmed · Haemoglobin structure, switching & haemoglobinopathies

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemoglobin is a tetramer of globin chains, each carrying haem. Developmental globin switching matches oxygen affinity to fetal and postnatal physiology: embryonic haemoglobins appear early, HbF (α2γ2) dominates fetal life, and HbA (α2β2) becomes dominant after birth; HbA2 (α2δ2) remains a small adult fraction.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Qualitative haemoglobinopathies change globin structure (for example HbS or HbC), whereas thalassaemias reduce synthesis of a globin chain, creating chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Severe β-thalassaemia causes profound marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure and increased HbF because β-chain production is absent or markedly reduced.

Globin gene abnormality → abnormal structure OR reduced chain synthesis → unstable/imbalanced Hb → ineffective erythropoiesis ± haemolysis → anaemia and systemic complications

3. Why this answer follows

HbF is α2γ2 and has higher oxygen affinity than HbA because it binds 2,3-BPG less strongly. This facilitates transfer of oxygen from maternal to fetal blood across the placenta. At birth HbF therefore predominates; the diagnosis of Down syndrome does not change the normal identity of the dominant cord-blood haemoglobin.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 2alpha/2beta globin chains why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. 2alpha/2delta globin chains why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. 2alpha/2gamma globin chains correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. 2gamma/2beta globin chains why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
E. 2gamma/2gamma globin chain why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Always ask whether the defect is qualitative (structure) or quantitative (amount). Hb electrophoresis patterns then become logical rather than rote.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 17
Outdated / corrected
Which of the ff percentages of blast in the bone marrow defines acute myeloid leukemia
AI
Answer: No valid option — No listed option is correct — the slide criterion is >20% blasts.
Outdated / corrected · Acute leukaemia: clonal biology, marrow failure & blast thresholds

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal haematopoiesis requires progenitor proliferation to remain coupled to orderly differentiation and apoptosis. Mature cells leave marrow while immature blasts remain a small minority.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Acute leukaemia arises when acquired genetic lesions give a clone increased proliferation/survival and—critically—a block in differentiation. Blasts accumulate in marrow, replacing normal haematopoiesis. Anaemia causes fatigue, neutropenia causes infection, thrombocytopenia causes bleeding, and high circulating blast counts can cause leukostasis. Prof. Der’s current slides use >20% blasts in blood or marrow as the general diagnostic threshold, with genetically defined exceptions; the older FAB system used 30%.

Driver mutations → ↑proliferation + ↓apoptosis + maturation arrest → blast accumulation → marrow replacement → cytopenias ± leukostasis/infiltration

3. Why this answer follows

The modern general threshold taught in Prof. Der's slides is >20% blasts in blood or marrow for acute leukaemia, with specific genetic exceptions that allow diagnosis below 20%. An option of 30% reflects the older FAB criterion. When none of the listed options gives 20%, the item is outdated/malformed rather than a reason to memorize the old threshold as current truth.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 30.0% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
B. 2.0% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
C. 10% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
D. 15% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
E. 12% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A high total WBC does not mean effective immunity: the cells may be malignant blasts. Also watch for old 30% FAB-threshold questions that conflict with current >20% criteria.

6. Question-quality note

Prof Der’s Block 3.7 slide defines acute leukaemia as >20% blasts in blood or marrow; 30% is the older FAB threshold. Some genetically defined AMLs can be diagnosed below 20%.

Source basis: Block 3.7, p. 260. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 18
Confirmed
The clinical features of multiple myeloma include?
AI
Answer: D — All of the above
Confirmed · Multiple myeloma: plasma-cell clonality, bone disease & paraproteins

1. Start with the normal physiology

Plasma cells are terminally differentiated B cells specialized for antibody secretion. Normal bone remodelling balances osteoclast-mediated resorption with osteoblast-mediated formation, while kidneys filter small proteins and maintain calcium homeostasis.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Multiple myeloma is a clonal plasma-cell neoplasm producing a monoclonal immunoglobulin or light chain. Marrow replacement causes usually normocytic anaemia; myeloma-cell signalling stimulates osteoclasts and suppresses osteoblast activity, causing punched-out osteolytic lesions, fractures and hypercalcaemia. Filtered light chains can injure tubules and form casts, contributing to renal failure; monoclonal light chains can also form AL amyloid. Suppression of normal immunoglobulins predisposes to recurrent infections.

Clonal plasma cells → M-protein/light chains + marrow infiltration + osteoclast activation → CRAB: hyperCalcaemia, Renal injury, Anaemia, Bone lesions

3. Why this answer follows

Myeloma can produce multiple apparently unrelated organ manifestations through one clonal plasma-cell process. Monoclonal light chains can deposit as AL amyloid, causing nephrotic syndrome and restrictive cardiomyopathy. Suppression of normal immunoglobulins predisposes to recurrent bacterial respiratory infection and chronic airway damage such as bronchiectasis. Therefore the systemic features can all coexist.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Bronchiectasis why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. Restrictive cardiomyopathy why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. Nephrotic syndrome why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. All of the above correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A lytic skull lesion, very high ESR/rouleaux, M-spike or Bence Jones protein should all converge on the same plasma-cell pathophysiology.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 363–368. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 19
Confirmed + note
The following are true of blood group O except?
AI
Answer: B — the false statement is that group O RBCs “lack A and B antibodies.”
Confirmed + note · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Group O RBCs lack A and B antigens; group O plasma contains both anti-A and anti-B. ‘Universal donor’ applies mainly to packed O RBCs—especially O-negative in emergencies—not to unrestricted transfusion of whole group O blood, because donor plasma antibodies can matter. Any statement that puts anti-A/anti-B ‘on the RBC’ confuses antigen with antibody.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Can be transfused into any patient why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. Blood group O RBC lack A and B antibodies correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. Can receive only O blood why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. Can be given to a neonate why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

Red cells carry antigens, not ABO antibodies. Group O RBCs lack A and B antigens; group O plasma contains anti-A and anti-B.

Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 20
Confirmed + note
The components of a standard cross-match does not include
AI
Answer: D — Indirect Coombs test.
Confirmed + note · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Pretransfusion compatibility work includes ABO/Rh typing and an antibody screen, followed by compatibility testing/crossmatch. The indirect antiglobulin (Coombs) test is a laboratory method used to detect IgG antibodies in these processes rather than a separate conceptual component parallel to ABO, Rh and antibody screening.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. ABO group why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. Rh type why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
C. Antibody screen for atypical antibodies why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. Indirect Coombs test correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

ABO/Rh typing, antibody screening and compatibility testing are components of pretransfusion testing; the indirect antiglobulin test is a method used within antibody screening/crossmatching rather than a separate standard “component.”

Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2025 · Question 21
Ambiguous item
The following are common causes of haemolytic disease of the newborn
AI
Answer: B — Rh (best single answer).
Ambiguous item · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Haemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn occurs when maternal IgG alloantibodies cross the placenta and bind fetal RBC antigens. Rh(D) is the classic major antigen; Kell can also cause severe disease partly by suppressing fetal erythropoiesis. ABO antibodies may also cause disease. The key is maternal sensitization to a fetal RBC antigen absent from the mother.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Kell why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. Rh correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. I why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. MN why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

Rh(D) incompatibility is a classic major cause of haemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn; Kell can also cause severe disease, so “common causes” makes the item slightly broad.

Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2025 · Question 23
Confirmed
Infectious causes of lymphocytosis does not include?
AI
Answer: C — Malaria
Confirmed · Leukocyte physiology, lymphocyte biology & reactive counts

1. Start with the normal physiology

Neutrophils arise in marrow through myeloblast → promyelocyte → myelocyte → metamyelocyte → band → segmented stages, with a large marrow storage pool allowing rapid release. B cells mature in marrow, T cells in thymus, and antigen-driven immune responses occur in secondary lymphoid tissues such as lymph nodes, spleen and mucosal lymphoid tissue. Mature helper T cells are typically CD4-positive/CD8-negative and coordinate immune responses through cytokines.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Leukocyte counts change through altered production, release, margination, tissue migration or destruction. Reactive lymphocytosis accompanies selected infections such as pertussis, infectious mononucleosis, measles and hepatitis. Immune neutropenia can occur when antibodies or immune dysregulation increase peripheral neutrophil destruction, as in Felty syndrome.

Stimulus/disease → altered marrow production or peripheral distribution/destruction → characteristic leukocyte-count pattern

3. Why this answer follows

The correct option best matches the normal physiology and the disease mechanism being tested; the remaining options either belong to a different pathway, describe a downstream association incorrectly, or do not fit the stem's biologic context.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Pertussis why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. Measles why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. Malaria correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Hepatitis why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Differentiate a quantitative disorder (too many/too few cells) from a functional disorder (cells present but defective). Several distractors are neutrophil-function syndromes, not causes of neutropenia.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 211–228. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. Very motile correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Differentiate a quantitative disorder (too many/too few cells) from a functional disorder (cells present but defective). Several distractors are neutrophil-function syndromes, not causes of neutropenia.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 211–228. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 26
Confirmed
The following are secondary lymphoid organs except?
AI
Answer: B — Thymus
Confirmed · Leukocyte physiology, lymphocyte biology & reactive counts

1. Start with the normal physiology

Neutrophils arise in marrow through myeloblast → promyelocyte → myelocyte → metamyelocyte → band → segmented stages, with a large marrow storage pool allowing rapid release. B cells mature in marrow, T cells in thymus, and antigen-driven immune responses occur in secondary lymphoid tissues such as lymph nodes, spleen and mucosal lymphoid tissue. Mature helper T cells are typically CD4-positive/CD8-negative and coordinate immune responses through cytokines.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Leukocyte counts change through altered production, release, margination, tissue migration or destruction. Reactive lymphocytosis accompanies selected infections such as pertussis, infectious mononucleosis, measles and hepatitis. Immune neutropenia can occur when antibodies or immune dysregulation increase peripheral neutrophil destruction, as in Felty syndrome.

Stimulus/disease → altered marrow production or peripheral distribution/destruction → characteristic leukocyte-count pattern

3. Why this answer follows

Primary lymphoid organs are where lymphocytes develop and undergo central selection: bone marrow for B cells and thymus for T cells. Secondary organs are where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and mount immune responses: lymph nodes, spleen and mucosal lymphoid tissues such as Peyer patches. Hence thymus is the exception.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Spleen why not / analysis
This is a secondary lymphoid organ/tissue where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and mount immune responses.
B. Thymus correct
Correct. The thymus is a primary lymphoid organ where T cells mature and undergo selection.
C. Lymph nodes why not / analysis
This is a secondary lymphoid organ/tissue where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and mount immune responses.
D. Peyer’s patches why not / analysis
This is a secondary lymphoid organ/tissue where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and mount immune responses.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Differentiate a quantitative disorder (too many/too few cells) from a functional disorder (cells present but defective). Several distractors are neutrophil-function syndromes, not causes of neutropenia.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 27
Confirmed
Enlargement of which of the following organs or in a disease condition tissue may lead to anaemia without blood lost externally?
AI
Answer: C — Spleen
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

The spleen normally filters RBCs and temporarily pools blood cells. When enlarged and hyperfunctional, transit time and macrophage exposure increase, so RBCs, WBCs and platelets may be sequestered and destroyed—hypersplenism. Anaemia can therefore occur without external blood loss because cells are removed internally from the circulation.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Kidney why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.
B. Liver why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.
C. Spleen correct
Correct. This is a secondary lymphoid organ/tissue where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and mount immune responses.
D. Heart why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 28
Confirmed
The following are true of hereditary spherocytosis except?
AI
Answer: B — It is caused by intrinsic defects in the protein involved in the horizontal interaction between the red cell membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer of the red blood cell
Confirmed · Haemolysis, splenic physiology & red-cell membrane disease

1. Start with the normal physiology

RBCs must repeatedly deform through narrow splenic cords and sinusoids. Normal membrane–cytoskeleton architecture preserves the biconcave shape, surface-area-to-volume ratio and flexibility needed for this passage. Senescent or abnormal cells are removed mainly by splenic and hepatic macrophages, while haem is converted to bilirubin and iron is recycled.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Extravascular haemolysis occurs when RBCs become poorly deformable or antibody-coated and are trapped and phagocytosed in spleen/liver/marrow. The result is anaemia, reticulocytosis, unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, jaundice and often splenomegaly. In hereditary spherocytosis, defects in vertical interactions between membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer cause progressive membrane loss; cells become spherical, lose deformability and are selectively destroyed in the spleen. Chronic bilirubin overproduction predisposes to pigment gallstones.

Membrane/shape abnormality → ↓deformability → splenic trapping → macrophage phagocytosis → extravascular haemolysis → anaemia + jaundice + splenomegaly + pigment stones

3. Why this answer follows

The core lesion is loss of membrane surface area due to defects in vertical anchoring between the lipid bilayer and membrane skeleton—commonly involving ankyrin, spectrin, band 3 or protein 4.2. A sphere has the lowest surface-area-to-volume ratio and cannot deform well in the splenic microcirculation. Horizontal cytoskeletal defects are more classically associated with elliptocytosis, making that statement the false one in the classic ‘except’ item.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. It is caused by intrinsic defects in the protein involved in thevertical interaction between the red cell membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer of the red blood cell why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. It is caused by intrinsic defects in the protein involved in the horizontal interaction between the red cell membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer of the red blood cell correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. An autosomal dominant inheritance pattern is seen in about 75% of cases. why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
D. The remaining 25% of the patients have compound heterozygosity why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Distinguish site of destruction from cause. “Spleen” is not merely enlarged—it is the mechanical filter that exposes poorly deformable cells.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 102–105. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 29
Ambiguous item
The following are known examples of qualitative haemoglobinopathies except?
AI
Answer: D — “Hb F disease” (best single answer).
Ambiguous item · Haemoglobin structure, switching & haemoglobinopathies

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemoglobin is a tetramer of globin chains, each carrying haem. Developmental globin switching matches oxygen affinity to fetal and postnatal physiology: embryonic haemoglobins appear early, HbF (α2γ2) dominates fetal life, and HbA (α2β2) becomes dominant after birth; HbA2 (α2δ2) remains a small adult fraction.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Qualitative haemoglobinopathies change globin structure (for example HbS or HbC), whereas thalassaemias reduce synthesis of a globin chain, creating chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Severe β-thalassaemia causes profound marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure and increased HbF because β-chain production is absent or markedly reduced.

Globin gene abnormality → abnormal structure OR reduced chain synthesis → unstable/imbalanced Hb → ineffective erythropoiesis ± haemolysis → anaemia and systemic complications

3. Why this answer follows

A qualitative haemoglobinopathy changes the structure of globin and produces an abnormal haemoglobin molecule, as in HbS, HbC or HbE. Thalassaemia is primarily quantitative—reduced synthesis of a globin chain. Compound states such as HbC/β-thalassaemia combine structural and quantitative defects, which is why some old items are imperfectly worded.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Hb C disease why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. Hb E disease why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. Hb C- thal disease why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
D. Hb F disease correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Always ask whether the defect is qualitative (structure) or quantitative (amount). Hb electrophoresis patterns then become logical rather than rote.

6. Question-quality note

HbF is a normal haemoglobin. Strictly, HbC-thalassaemia is a combined qualitative/quantitative haemoglobinopathy, so the item is not perfectly framed.

Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 128, 132. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 30
Confirmed + note
The following may complicate hereditary spherocytosis except?
AI
Answer: A — Severe haemolytic anaemia (best “except” answer).
Confirmed + note · Haemolysis, splenic physiology & red-cell membrane disease

1. Start with the normal physiology

RBCs must repeatedly deform through narrow splenic cords and sinusoids. Normal membrane–cytoskeleton architecture preserves the biconcave shape, surface-area-to-volume ratio and flexibility needed for this passage. Senescent or abnormal cells are removed mainly by splenic and hepatic macrophages, while haem is converted to bilirubin and iron is recycled.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Extravascular haemolysis occurs when RBCs become poorly deformable or antibody-coated and are trapped and phagocytosed in spleen/liver/marrow. The result is anaemia, reticulocytosis, unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, jaundice and often splenomegaly. In hereditary spherocytosis, defects in vertical interactions between membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer cause progressive membrane loss; cells become spherical, lose deformability and are selectively destroyed in the spleen. Chronic bilirubin overproduction predisposes to pigment gallstones.

Membrane/shape abnormality → ↓deformability → splenic trapping → macrophage phagocytosis → extravascular haemolysis → anaemia + jaundice + splenomegaly + pigment stones

3. Why this answer follows

The core lesion is loss of membrane surface area due to defects in vertical anchoring between the lipid bilayer and membrane skeleton—commonly involving ankyrin, spectrin, band 3 or protein 4.2. A sphere has the lowest surface-area-to-volume ratio and cannot deform well in the splenic microcirculation. Horizontal cytoskeletal defects are more classically associated with elliptocytosis, making that statement the false one in the classic ‘except’ item.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Severe haemolytic anaemia correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. Mild jaundice why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. Cholelithiasis why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
D. Moderate splenic enlargement why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Distinguish site of destruction from cause. “Spleen” is not merely enlarged—it is the mechanical filter that exposes poorly deformable cells.

6. Question-quality note

Prof Der describes the usual phenotype as mild–moderate anaemia with mild jaundice, pigment gallstones and moderate splenic enlargement.

Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 102–105. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2025 · Question 31
Confirmed
The following are associated clinical features of Cooley’s anaemia except?
AI
Answer: A — It is a disease adulthood
Confirmed · Haemoglobin structure, switching & haemoglobinopathies

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemoglobin is a tetramer of globin chains, each carrying haem. Developmental globin switching matches oxygen affinity to fetal and postnatal physiology: embryonic haemoglobins appear early, HbF (α2γ2) dominates fetal life, and HbA (α2β2) becomes dominant after birth; HbA2 (α2δ2) remains a small adult fraction.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Qualitative haemoglobinopathies change globin structure (for example HbS or HbC), whereas thalassaemias reduce synthesis of a globin chain, creating chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Severe β-thalassaemia causes profound marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure and increased HbF because β-chain production is absent or markedly reduced.

Globin gene abnormality → abnormal structure OR reduced chain synthesis → unstable/imbalanced Hb → ineffective erythropoiesis ± haemolysis → anaemia and systemic complications

3. Why this answer follows

Cooley anaemia is β-thalassaemia major. After the physiologic postnatal fall in γ-chain/HbF, the infant cannot make adequate β chains, causing severe chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Marrow expansion causes skeletal changes; chronic anaemia causes growth retardation. Electrophoresis shows markedly increased HbF, often increased HbA2, and very low/absent HbA depending on the β-thalassaemia genotype.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. It is a disease adulthood correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. It is associated with extravascular haemolysis why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. Thinning of the cortical bone why not / analysis
Bone itself is not the classic target; bone marrow is affected because haematopoietic precursors divide rapidly.
D. Growth retardation is a common feature why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Always ask whether the defect is qualitative (structure) or quantitative (amount). Hb electrophoresis patterns then become logical rather than rote.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 151–155. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
