2024 · Question 30
Confirmed
Oral iron is absorbed in
AI
Answer: C — 1st part of duodenum
Confirmed · Iron physiology, absorption & iron-deficiency anaemia

1. Start with the normal physiology

Iron is essential for haem synthesis. Dietary non-haem iron is best absorbed in the duodenum/proximal small bowel after reduction to the ferrous form; haem iron is absorbed more efficiently. Enterocyte iron can be stored in ferritin or exported through ferroportin to plasma transferrin. Hepcidin from the liver is the master negative regulator: it binds ferroportin and promotes its internalization, reducing intestinal absorption and macrophage iron release.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Iron deficiency first depletes stores (falling ferritin), then reduces circulating available iron and transferrin saturation, and finally limits haemoglobin synthesis. Erythroblasts undergo extra divisions while trying to reach an adequate intracellular haemoglobin concentration, producing microcytic, hypochromic cells. Chronic blood loss—especially gastrointestinal blood loss in older adults—is a classic mechanism.

↓Iron stores → ↓haem synthesis → delayed haemoglobinization → extra erythroblast divisions → microcytosis + hypochromia → reduced O₂ carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

The duodenum is optimized for iron uptake through apical transport systems and regulation by systemic iron need. As luminal contents pass distally, iron availability and uptake decline. Therefore proximal duodenum is the exam answer even though some absorption also occurs in proximal jejunum.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. stomach why not / analysis
The stomach prepares nutrients and secretes intrinsic factor, but it is not the principal site of iron or B12 absorption asked here.
B. jejenum why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. 1st part of duodenum correct
Correct. The proximal duodenum is the principal site of iron absorption.
D. 2nd part of duodenum why not / analysis
The proximal duodenum is the principal site of iron absorption.
E. colon why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Know both the anatomy (duodenum) and the regulatory physiology (hepcidin–ferroportin). “Iron absorbed where?” and “why microcytic?” are two faces of the same pathway.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 71–74. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 31
Confirmed
The following are co-factors for haemoglobin synthesis except
AI
Answer: A — Folate
Confirmed · Folate one-carbon metabolism & neural-tube biology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Folate is converted to tetrahydrofolate derivatives that shuttle one-carbon units needed for purine synthesis and conversion of deoxyuridylate to thymidylate. This is crucial for DNA replication in rapidly proliferating tissues. Body stores are limited compared with B12, so deficiency can develop relatively quickly when intake is poor or requirements rise.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Deficiency slows DNA synthesis, producing megaloblastic change in marrow and mucosa. During very early embryogenesis, rapid proliferation is required for neural-tube closure; inadequate folate availability increases the risk of neural-tube defects. Folate can correct the haematologic abnormality of B12 deficiency while allowing neurologic injury to progress, which is why B12 deficiency must be excluded before treating an unexplained megaloblastic anaemia with folate alone.

↓Folate/THF one-carbon transfer → ↓thymidylate/purines → defective DNA synthesis → megaloblastosis; in embryo → impaired neural-tube closure

3. Why this answer follows

Prof. Der separates essential nutritional factors (iron, B12, folate) from a list of cofactors such as manganese, cobalt and several vitamins. Old questions sometimes mix these categories. Calcium is not part of the listed erythropoietic/haemoglobin-synthesis cofactor set; zinc is also not on the specific slide list.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Folate correct
Correct. Folate is an essential nutritional factor for DNA synthesis; in Prof. Der's slide taxonomy it is listed separately from the ‘cofactor’ group.
B. Cobalt why not / analysis
This appears on Prof. Der's listed haemoglobin/erythropoietic cofactor set.
C. Manganese why not / analysis
This appears on Prof. Der's listed haemoglobin/erythropoietic cofactor set.
D. Pyridoxine why not / analysis
This appears on Prof. Der's listed haemoglobin/erythropoietic cofactor set.
E. Riboflavin why not / analysis
This appears on Prof. Der's listed haemoglobin/erythropoietic cofactor set.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Link “folate” to DNA synthesis, not just to a memorized disease name. That mechanism explains marrow, GI and fetal manifestations.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 32
Ambiguous item
The following may complicate hereditary spherocytosis
AI
Answer: Ambiguous — Question is malformed as written. Cholelithiasis is the classic complication; mild jaundice and moderate splenomegaly are characteristic features. Massive splenomegaly is not characteristic.
Ambiguous item · Haemolysis, splenic physiology & red-cell membrane disease

1. Start with the normal physiology

RBCs must repeatedly deform through narrow splenic cords and sinusoids. Normal membrane–cytoskeleton architecture preserves the biconcave shape, surface-area-to-volume ratio and flexibility needed for this passage. Senescent or abnormal cells are removed mainly by splenic and hepatic macrophages, while haem is converted to bilirubin and iron is recycled.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Extravascular haemolysis occurs when RBCs become poorly deformable or antibody-coated and are trapped and phagocytosed in spleen/liver/marrow. The result is anaemia, reticulocytosis, unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, jaundice and often splenomegaly. In hereditary spherocytosis, defects in vertical interactions between membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer cause progressive membrane loss; cells become spherical, lose deformability and are selectively destroyed in the spleen. Chronic bilirubin overproduction predisposes to pigment gallstones.

Membrane/shape abnormality → ↓deformability → splenic trapping → macrophage phagocytosis → extravascular haemolysis → anaemia + jaundice + splenomegaly + pigment stones

3. Why this answer follows

The core lesion is loss of membrane surface area due to defects in vertical anchoring between the lipid bilayer and membrane skeleton—commonly involving ankyrin, spectrin, band 3 or protein 4.2. A sphere has the lowest surface-area-to-volume ratio and cannot deform well in the splenic microcirculation. Horizontal cytoskeletal defects are more classically associated with elliptocytosis, making that statement the false one in the classic ‘except’ item.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Severe hemolytic anemia why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
B. Mild jaundice why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
C. Cholilithiasis why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
D. Moderate splenic enlargement why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
E. Massive splenic enlargement why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Distinguish site of destruction from cause. “Spleen” is not merely enlarged—it is the mechanical filter that exposes poorly deformable cells.

6. Question-quality note

Prof Der describes mild–moderate anaemia, mild jaundice, pigment gallstones and moderate splenic enlargement in hereditary spherocytosis.

Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 102–105. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 33
Confirmed
Pel Ebstein fever is common in which of the following hematologic malignancies?
AI
Answer: B — Nodular sclerosing Hodgkins lymphoma
Confirmed · Hodgkin lymphoma, EBV risk & classic clinical associations

1. Start with the normal physiology

Germinal-center B cells that fail appropriate selection normally undergo apoptosis. Immune-cell interactions within lymph nodes are tightly regulated by cytokine networks.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Hodgkin lymphoma contains a minority of neoplastic Reed–Sternberg cells within a prominent reactive inflammatory background. In a subset, EBV contributes to oncogenic signalling and survival; prior infectious mononucleosis therefore increases later Hodgkin-lymphoma risk. Clinical disease often presents with painless lymphadenopathy and constitutional ‘B symptoms’; Pel–Ebstein fever is a classic historical association.

Susceptible B cell ± EBV-driven survival signalling → Reed–Sternberg clone → cytokine-rich inflammatory microenvironment → lymphadenopathy/B symptoms

3. Why this answer follows

Pel–Ebstein fever describes a historical pattern of cyclical fever classically associated with Hodgkin lymphoma. It is neither sensitive nor specific enough to diagnose Hodgkin lymphoma, but it remains a classic exam association.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Burkitts lymphoma why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
B. Nodular sclerosing Hodgkins lymphoma correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
D. Non Hodgkins lymphoma why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
E. Chronic myeloid leukemia why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 EBV/infectious mononucleosis is an association, not a statement that every Hodgkin lymphoma is EBV-positive.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 356–360. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 34
Confirmed
About Blood group O:
AI
Answer: B — Can be transfused in any patient
Confirmed · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Group O RBCs lack A and B antigens; group O plasma contains both anti-A and anti-B. ‘Universal donor’ applies mainly to packed O RBCs—especially O-negative in emergencies—not to unrestricted transfusion of whole group O blood, because donor plasma antibodies can matter. Any statement that puts anti-A/anti-B ‘on the RBC’ confuses antigen with antibody.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Has both anti A and Anti B antigens why not / analysis
Both antibodies are characteristic of group O plasma, not group B plasma.
B. Can be transfused in any patient correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. First blood group to be identified in humans why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. Less common in Africa why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.
Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 35
Confirmed
The commonest haemoglobin found in cord blood of a baby born with down syndrome is
AI
Answer: C — 2alpha 2gamma
Confirmed · Haemoglobin structure, switching & haemoglobinopathies

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemoglobin is a tetramer of globin chains, each carrying haem. Developmental globin switching matches oxygen affinity to fetal and postnatal physiology: embryonic haemoglobins appear early, HbF (α2γ2) dominates fetal life, and HbA (α2β2) becomes dominant after birth; HbA2 (α2δ2) remains a small adult fraction.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Qualitative haemoglobinopathies change globin structure (for example HbS or HbC), whereas thalassaemias reduce synthesis of a globin chain, creating chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Severe β-thalassaemia causes profound marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure and increased HbF because β-chain production is absent or markedly reduced.

Globin gene abnormality → abnormal structure OR reduced chain synthesis → unstable/imbalanced Hb → ineffective erythropoiesis ± haemolysis → anaemia and systemic complications

3. Why this answer follows

HbF is α2γ2 and has higher oxygen affinity than HbA because it binds 2,3-BPG less strongly. This facilitates transfer of oxygen from maternal to fetal blood across the placenta. At birth HbF therefore predominates; the diagnosis of Down syndrome does not change the normal identity of the dominant cord-blood haemoglobin.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 2alpha 2beta why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. 2alpha 2delta why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. 2alpha 2gamma correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. 2gamma 2beta why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
E. 2gamma 2gamma why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Always ask whether the defect is qualitative (structure) or quantitative (amount). Hb electrophoresis patterns then become logical rather than rote.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 36
Confirmed + note
The purpose of major cross-match in transfusion
AI
Answer: D — Detect recipient antibodies/incompatibility against donor red-cell antigens — thereby ensuring compatible, safe blood.
Confirmed + note · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Choose the option that states detection of atypical/recipient antibodies against donor RBC antigens. In versions where that option is garbled, that is the intended concept. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. identify donor blood group why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. identify patient blood group why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
C. give safe blood why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. detect typical antibodies directed against foreign antibodies correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
E. prevent haemolytic disease of newborn why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

Choose the option that states detection of atypical/recipient antibodies against donor RBC antigens. In versions where that option is garbled, that is the intended concept.

2024 · Question 37
Confirmed
What is the common clinical presentation of hemophilia in affected individuals
AI
Answer: D — haemathrosis
Confirmed · Haemophilia physiology, genetics & factor assays

1. Start with the normal physiology

Factor VIII acts as a cofactor with activated factor IX on phospholipid surfaces to accelerate factor X activation in the intrinsic tenase complex. This amplification is important for generating the thrombin burst needed for a stable fibrin clot. vWF stabilizes circulating factor VIII.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Haemophilia A is factor VIII deficiency and haemophilia B is factor IX deficiency; both are usually X-linked recessive and clinically produce deep bleeding, large haematomas and haemarthroses rather than petechiae. Screening shows prolonged aPTT with normal PT, but definitive diagnosis and distinction between A and B require specific factor assays. Mild haemophilia A can respond to desmopressin, which raises endogenous vWF and factor VIII.

↓VIII or ↓IX → impaired intrinsic tenase → inadequate factor X/thrombin burst → weak fibrin clot → delayed/deep bleeding and haemarthroses

3. Why this answer follows

Coagulation-factor deficiency allows the initial platelet plug to form but impairs stable fibrin reinforcement. Bleeding therefore tends to be delayed, deep and recurrent—especially haemarthroses and muscle haematomas—rather than the immediate mucocutaneous petechial pattern of platelet disorders.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. prolong bleeding time why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. petechial haemorrhages why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. bleeding gum why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
D. haemathrosis correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
E. anaemia why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 aPTT tells you the pathway is abnormal; the factor assay tells you which disease. Do not confuse a screening test with a definitive diagnosis.
Source basis: Pathology Secrets / haemostasis principles. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 38
Ambiguous item
Peripheral blood film from a 78-year-old woman diagnosed of adenocarcinoma of the colon will show all of the following except?
AI
Answer: D — D is the best “except” answer — microcytic normochromic cells are not the classic iron-deficiency film pattern.
Ambiguous item · Iron physiology, absorption & iron-deficiency anaemia

1. Start with the normal physiology

Iron is essential for haem synthesis. Dietary non-haem iron is best absorbed in the duodenum/proximal small bowel after reduction to the ferrous form; haem iron is absorbed more efficiently. Enterocyte iron can be stored in ferritin or exported through ferroportin to plasma transferrin. Hepcidin from the liver is the master negative regulator: it binds ferroportin and promotes its internalization, reducing intestinal absorption and macrophage iron release.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Iron deficiency first depletes stores (falling ferritin), then reduces circulating available iron and transferrin saturation, and finally limits haemoglobin synthesis. Erythroblasts undergo extra divisions while trying to reach an adequate intracellular haemoglobin concentration, producing microcytic, hypochromic cells. Chronic blood loss—especially gastrointestinal blood loss in older adults—is a classic mechanism.

↓Iron stores → ↓haem synthesis → delayed haemoglobinization → extra erythroblast divisions → microcytosis + hypochromia → reduced O₂ carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

Screening coagulation tests identify which part of haemostasis is abnormal, but definitive diagnosis requires measurement of the relevant factor activity. Haemophilia A and B can look identical clinically and both prolong aPTT; only factor VIII versus IX assays distinguish them reliably.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Anisocytosis why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. Poikilocytosis why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. Normochromic normocytic red blood cells why not / analysis
This pattern fits many non-iron-deficiency anaemias or early/mixed disease and is not the classic fully developed iron-deficiency film.
D. Microcytic normochromic red blood cells correct
Correct. Microcytosis from iron-restricted haemoglobin synthesis is typically accompanied by hypochromia; ‘microcytic normochromic’ is not the classic pattern.
E. Microcytic hypochromic red blood cells why not / analysis
This is the classic red-cell morphology when haemoglobin synthesis is limited by iron deficiency.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Know both the anatomy (duodenum) and the regulatory physiology (hepcidin–ferroportin). “Iron absorbed where?” and “why microcytic?” are two faces of the same pathway.

6. Question-quality note

Colon cancer commonly causes chronic occult blood loss with iron deficiency: microcytosis, hypochromia, anisocytosis and poikilocytosis. Normocytic cells can occur early or in mixed anaemia, making the wording imperfect.

Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 59, 74. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 39
Confirmed
Each unit of platelet will raise the count by?
AI
Answer: A — 5,000-10,000 cells/L
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

A standard random-donor platelet unit contains enough platelets to raise an average adult count by roughly 5,000–10,000/µL under ideal conditions. The observed increment may be lower with fever, sepsis, DIC, splenomegaly or alloimmune refractoriness.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 5,000-10,000 cells/L correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. 2,000-3,000 cells/L why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. 2,500-4,000cells/L why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
D. 500-1000cells/L E. 20-700cells/L why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
Source basis: Pathology Secrets / haemostasis principles. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2024 · Question 40
Confirmed
Which of the following ratio is true of T-cells in bone marrow of a 12 year old male?
AI
Answer: B — CD4+ : CD8+ = 1:2
Confirmed · Leukocyte physiology, lymphocyte biology & reactive counts

1. Start with the normal physiology

Neutrophils arise in marrow through myeloblast → promyelocyte → myelocyte → metamyelocyte → band → segmented stages, with a large marrow storage pool allowing rapid release. B cells mature in marrow, T cells in thymus, and antigen-driven immune responses occur in secondary lymphoid tissues such as lymph nodes, spleen and mucosal lymphoid tissue. Mature helper T cells are typically CD4-positive/CD8-negative and coordinate immune responses through cytokines.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Leukocyte counts change through altered production, release, margination, tissue migration or destruction. Reactive lymphocytosis accompanies selected infections such as pertussis, infectious mononucleosis, measles and hepatitis. Immune neutropenia can occur when antibodies or immune dysregulation increase peripheral neutrophil destruction, as in Felty syndrome.

Stimulus/disease → altered marrow production or peripheral distribution/destruction → characteristic leukocyte-count pattern

3. Why this answer follows

Prof Der gives a bone-marrow CD4+:CD8+ ratio of 1:2. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. CD4+ : CD8+ = 2:1 why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. CD4+ : CD8+ = 1:2 correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. CD4+ : CD8+ = 1:1 why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. CD4+ : CD8+ = 2:2 E. CD4+ : CD8+ = 1.2:1 why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Differentiate a quantitative disorder (too many/too few cells) from a functional disorder (cells present but defective). Several distractors are neutrophil-function syndromes, not causes of neutropenia.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 211–228. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2023

9 questions
You
2023 · Question 1
Confirmed
Which of the following is the fastest on the haemoglobin electrophoresis
AI
Answer: A — HbA
Confirmed · Haemoglobin structure, switching & haemoglobinopathies

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemoglobin is a tetramer of globin chains, each carrying haem. Developmental globin switching matches oxygen affinity to fetal and postnatal physiology: embryonic haemoglobins appear early, HbF (α2γ2) dominates fetal life, and HbA (α2β2) becomes dominant after birth; HbA2 (α2δ2) remains a small adult fraction.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Qualitative haemoglobinopathies change globin structure (for example HbS or HbC), whereas thalassaemias reduce synthesis of a globin chain, creating chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Severe β-thalassaemia causes profound marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure and increased HbF because β-chain production is absent or markedly reduced.

Globin gene abnormality → abnormal structure OR reduced chain synthesis → unstable/imbalanced Hb → ineffective erythropoiesis ± haemolysis → anaemia and systemic complications

3. Why this answer follows

At alkaline pH, haemoglobins separate according to net negative charge. HbA has greater anodal mobility than HbF and HbA2 among the common options listed. The exact migration order depends on the electrophoretic medium and pH, which is why the testing condition matters.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. HbA correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. HbA2 why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. HbF why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
D. Gower why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Always ask whether the defect is qualitative (structure) or quantitative (amount). Hb electrophoresis patterns then become logical rather than rote.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2023 · Question 2
Ambiguous item
In determining the phenotype of ABO blood grouping,
AI
Answer: C — O is recessive (best single answer).
Ambiguous item · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

A and B alleles are codominant: AB individuals express both antigens. O is usually an amorphic/recessive allele, so AO phenotypes as A and BO phenotypes as B. Thus ‘O is recessive’ is the broad genetics rule, though ‘A is dominant over O’ is also true—making some versions of the item imperfectly single-best-answer.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. O is dominant over A why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. B is dominant over A why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
C. O is recessive correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. A is dominant over O why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

A and B are codominant and each is dominant over O, so option D (“A is dominant over O”) is also true. The item is not strictly single-best-answer.

Source basis: ABO genetics principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You

AI

Confirmed · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Blood group O is the most prevalent ABO group in Ghana/West African populations. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

2023 · Question 4
Confirmed
Which of the following blood groups is termed as universal donor
AI
Answer: D — Blood group O
Confirmed · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Packed group O RBCs carry neither A nor B antigen, minimizing immediate ABO antibody binding; O-negative cells additionally lack D antigen and are preferred for uncrossmatched emergency RBC transfusion. This does not mean group O whole blood is universally safe because donor plasma contains anti-A and anti-B.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Blood group A why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. Blood group B why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
C. Blood group AB why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. Blood group O correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.
Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2023 · Question 5
Confirmed
Which of the following blood groups is termed as universal recipient
AI
Answer: C — Blood group AB
Confirmed · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

An AB recipient lacks anti-A and anti-B in plasma, so ABO-wise they can receive A, B, AB or O RBCs. When Rh is included, AB-positive is the conventional universal RBC recipient because it also lacks anti-D risk from D-positive donor RBCs.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Blood group A why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. Blood group B why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
C. Blood group AB correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Blood group O why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

Confirmed
Which of the following blood groups is the rarest?
AI
Answer: B — AB negative
Confirmed · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

AB-negative is the rarest of the listed common ABO/Rh phenotypes. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. AB positive why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
B. AB negative correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. O positive why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.
D. O negative why not / analysis
This option confuses ABO antigens, plasma antibodies, typing, or compatibility testing. Re-check which compartment is being tested: donor RBC antigen versus recipient plasma antibody.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.
