Confirmed
An Hb electrophoresis of a child with Cooley’s anaemia will show the following except?
AI
Answer: C — Deceased HbF
Confirmed · Haemoglobin structure, switching & haemoglobinopathies

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemoglobin is a tetramer of globin chains, each carrying haem. Developmental globin switching matches oxygen affinity to fetal and postnatal physiology: embryonic haemoglobins appear early, HbF (α2γ2) dominates fetal life, and HbA (α2β2) becomes dominant after birth; HbA2 (α2δ2) remains a small adult fraction.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Qualitative haemoglobinopathies change globin structure (for example HbS or HbC), whereas thalassaemias reduce synthesis of a globin chain, creating chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Severe β-thalassaemia causes profound marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure and increased HbF because β-chain production is absent or markedly reduced.

Globin gene abnormality → abnormal structure OR reduced chain synthesis → unstable/imbalanced Hb → ineffective erythropoiesis ± haemolysis → anaemia and systemic complications

3. Why this answer follows

Cooley anaemia is β-thalassaemia major. After the physiologic postnatal fall in γ-chain/HbF, the infant cannot make adequate β chains, causing severe chain imbalance, ineffective erythropoiesis and haemolysis. Marrow expansion causes skeletal changes; chronic anaemia causes growth retardation. Electrophoresis shows markedly increased HbF, often increased HbA2, and very low/absent HbA depending on the β-thalassaemia genotype.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Elevated HbF why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. Elevated HbA2 why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. Deceased HbF correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Decreased HbA why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.

💡 Always ask whether the defect is qualitative (structure) or quantitative (amount). Hb electrophoresis patterns then become logical rather than rote.

2022 Parallel · Question 31
Confirmed
Which variant of the acute myeloid leukaemia is associated with DIC?
AI
Answer: B — AML3
Confirmed · Acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APL/M3), PML–RARA & DIC

1. Start with the normal physiology

Retinoic-acid receptor signalling is part of the transcriptional program that permits myeloid precursors to mature beyond the promyelocyte stage. Coagulation is normally localized to sites of injury and balanced by endogenous anticoagulant and fibrinolytic systems.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

APL classically carries t(15;17), generating PML–RARA and a profound differentiation block at the promyelocyte stage. Abnormal promyelocytes contain granule-associated procoagulant substances and can drive tissue-factor activity and hyperfibrinolysis. The result may be disseminated intravascular coagulation: widespread coagulation activation consumes platelets/factors while fibrinolysis degrades clot, producing simultaneous thrombosis and severe bleeding. ATRA targets the differentiation block by pharmacologically engaging the altered retinoic-acid pathway.

t(15;17) → PML–RARA → promyelocyte maturation arrest → procoagulant/fibrinolytic activation → DIC → bleeding + consumption coagulopathy

3. Why this answer follows

APL (FAB M3) is the AML subtype with the strongest DIC association. The t(15;17) PML–RARA lesion blocks promyelocyte differentiation. Abnormal promyelocytes provide potent procoagulant and fibrinolytic activity; cell lysis can intensify this, consuming platelets and fibrinogen while increasing fibrin degradation. Clinically, severe bleeding may be the first emergency.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. AML7 why not / analysis
FAB M7 is acute megakaryoblastic leukaemia, strongly associated with Down syndrome rather than the classic DIC syndrome.
B. AML3 correct
Correct. FAB M3 is acute promyelocytic leukaemia, classically associated with t(15;17) and DIC.
C. AML4e why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
D. AML6b why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 M3/APL should instantly connect to t(15;17), PML–RARA, bundles of Auer rods, DIC and differentiation therapy.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 260–275. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 32
Confirmed
Which variant of the acute myeloid leukaemia is associated with Down syndrome?
AI
Answer: A — AML7
Confirmed · Acute leukaemia: clonal biology, marrow failure & blast thresholds

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal haematopoiesis requires progenitor proliferation to remain coupled to orderly differentiation and apoptosis. Mature cells leave marrow while immature blasts remain a small minority.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Acute leukaemia arises when acquired genetic lesions give a clone increased proliferation/survival and—critically—a block in differentiation. Blasts accumulate in marrow, replacing normal haematopoiesis. Anaemia causes fatigue, neutropenia causes infection, thrombocytopenia causes bleeding, and high circulating blast counts can cause leukostasis. Prof. Der’s current slides use >20% blasts in blood or marrow as the general diagnostic threshold, with genetically defined exceptions; the older FAB system used 30%.

Driver mutations → ↑proliferation + ↓apoptosis + maturation arrest → blast accumulation → marrow replacement → cytopenias ± leukostasis/infiltration

3. Why this answer follows

Children with Down syndrome have a distinctive predisposition to myeloid neoplasia, particularly acute megakaryoblastic leukaemia (FAB M7), often preceded in neonates by transient abnormal myelopoiesis. The trisomy-21 genetic background cooperates with acquired mutations affecting megakaryocytic differentiation.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. AML7 correct
Correct. FAB M7 is acute megakaryoblastic leukaemia, strongly associated with Down syndrome rather than the classic DIC syndrome.
B. AML3 why not / analysis
FAB M3 is acute promyelocytic leukaemia, classically associated with t(15;17) and DIC.
C. AML4e why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
D. AML6b why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A high total WBC does not mean effective immunity: the cells may be malignant blasts. Also watch for old 30% FAB-threshold questions that conflict with current >20% criteria.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 260–275. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 33
Confirmed
Which of the following chemotherapeutic agents is BCR-ABL inhibitor?
AI
Answer: C — Imatinib
Confirmed · CML: BCR–ABL signalling, Philadelphia chromosome & targeted therapy

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABL is a tightly regulated tyrosine kinase involved in cellular signalling. Normal myeloid production remains dependent on growth-factor signals and preserves controlled proliferation.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

In CML, reciprocal t(9;22) creates the BCR–ABL fusion gene on the shortened chromosome 22 (Philadelphia chromosome). The fusion protein has constitutive tyrosine-kinase activity, continuously activating proliferative and survival pathways. Because the lesion arises in a pluripotent stem cell, multiple myeloid lineages are involved and maturation is largely preserved—hence marked leukocytosis with a spectrum of granulocytic precursors rather than only blasts. Imatinib inhibits BCR–ABL kinase activity and directly targets the driver lesion.

t(9;22) → BCR–ABL constitutive kinase → autonomous myeloid proliferation/survival → granulocytic leukocytosis + splenomegaly → possible blast crisis

3. Why this answer follows

BCR–ABL is the driver kinase in classic CML. Imatinib binds the ABL kinase domain and stabilizes an inactive conformation, preventing ATP-dependent phosphorylation of downstream targets. Because therapy targets the causal signalling lesion rather than nonspecifically killing all dividing cells, it can produce profound haematologic and cytogenetic responses.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Cyclosphosphamide why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
B. Hydroxyurea why not / analysis
Hydroxyurea can reduce cell counts by inhibiting DNA synthesis but does not specifically inhibit the BCR–ABL kinase.
C. Imatinib correct
Correct. Imatinib directly inhibits BCR–ABL tyrosine kinase and is the mechanism-based answer for classic CML.
D. Interferon why not / analysis
Interferon-α has historical activity in CML but is not a direct BCR–ABL kinase inhibitor.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Do not just memorize “9;22.” Know that the abnormal chromosome 22 is the Philadelphia chromosome and why a tyrosine-kinase inhibitor works.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 313–323. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 34
Outdated / corrected
Which of the following percentages of blasts in the bone marrow defines acute myeloid leukaemia?
AI
Answer: No valid option — No listed option is correct — the slide criterion is >20% blasts.
Outdated / corrected · Acute leukaemia: clonal biology, marrow failure & blast thresholds

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal haematopoiesis requires progenitor proliferation to remain coupled to orderly differentiation and apoptosis. Mature cells leave marrow while immature blasts remain a small minority.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Acute leukaemia arises when acquired genetic lesions give a clone increased proliferation/survival and—critically—a block in differentiation. Blasts accumulate in marrow, replacing normal haematopoiesis. Anaemia causes fatigue, neutropenia causes infection, thrombocytopenia causes bleeding, and high circulating blast counts can cause leukostasis. Prof. Der’s current slides use >20% blasts in blood or marrow as the general diagnostic threshold, with genetically defined exceptions; the older FAB system used 30%.

Driver mutations → ↑proliferation + ↓apoptosis + maturation arrest → blast accumulation → marrow replacement → cytopenias ± leukostasis/infiltration

3. Why this answer follows

The modern general threshold taught in Prof. Der's slides is >20% blasts in blood or marrow for acute leukaemia, with specific genetic exceptions that allow diagnosis below 20%. An option of 30% reflects the older FAB criterion. When none of the listed options gives 20%, the item is outdated/malformed rather than a reason to memorize the old threshold as current truth.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 30.0% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
B. 2.0% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
C. 10% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
D. 15% why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A high total WBC does not mean effective immunity: the cells may be malignant blasts. Also watch for old 30% FAB-threshold questions that conflict with current >20% criteria.

6. Question-quality note

Prof Der’s Block 3.7 slide defines acute leukaemia as >20% blasts in blood or marrow; 30% is the older FAB threshold. Some genetically defined AMLs can be diagnosed below 20%.

Source basis: Block 3.7, p. 260. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

Confirmed · Acute leukaemia: clonal biology, marrow failure & blast thresholds

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal haematopoiesis requires progenitor proliferation to remain coupled to orderly differentiation and apoptosis. Mature cells leave marrow while immature blasts remain a small minority.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Acute leukaemia arises when acquired genetic lesions give a clone increased proliferation/survival and—critically—a block in differentiation. Blasts accumulate in marrow, replacing normal haematopoiesis. Anaemia causes fatigue, neutropenia causes infection, thrombocytopenia causes bleeding, and high circulating blast counts can cause leukostasis. Prof. Der’s current slides use >20% blasts in blood or marrow as the general diagnostic threshold, with genetically defined exceptions; the older FAB system used 30%.

Driver mutations → ↑proliferation + ↓apoptosis + maturation arrest → blast accumulation → marrow replacement → cytopenias ± leukostasis/infiltration

3. Why this answer follows

Prof Der states AML incidence rises with age and peaks after 60 years. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

2022 Parallel · Question 36
Confirmed
In chronic myeloid leukaemia, the abnormal chromosome is
AI
Answer: A — T(9,22)
Confirmed · CML: BCR–ABL signalling, Philadelphia chromosome & targeted therapy

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABL is a tightly regulated tyrosine kinase involved in cellular signalling. Normal myeloid production remains dependent on growth-factor signals and preserves controlled proliferation.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

In CML, reciprocal t(9;22) creates the BCR–ABL fusion gene on the shortened chromosome 22 (Philadelphia chromosome). The fusion protein has constitutive tyrosine-kinase activity, continuously activating proliferative and survival pathways. Because the lesion arises in a pluripotent stem cell, multiple myeloid lineages are involved and maturation is largely preserved—hence marked leukocytosis with a spectrum of granulocytic precursors rather than only blasts. Imatinib inhibits BCR–ABL kinase activity and directly targets the driver lesion.

t(9;22) → BCR–ABL constitutive kinase → autonomous myeloid proliferation/survival → granulocytic leukocytosis + splenomegaly → possible blast crisis

3. Why this answer follows

The reciprocal translocation is t(9;22). The shortened derivative chromosome 22 is called the Philadelphia chromosome and contains the BCR–ABL fusion. Thus ‘22’ alone identifies the chromosome that is morphologically abnormal, while t(9;22) states the actual cytogenetic event; Prof. Der's wording determines which the MCQ is asking.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. T(9,22) correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. 22 why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
C. 9 why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
D. T(9,24) why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Do not just memorize “9;22.” Know that the abnormal chromosome 22 is the Philadelphia chromosome and why a tyrosine-kinase inhibitor works.

2022 Parallel · Question 37
Confirmed
The bony lesion that is commonly seen in multiple myeloma is?
AI
Answer: C — Osteolytic
Confirmed · Multiple myeloma: plasma-cell clonality, bone disease & paraproteins

1. Start with the normal physiology

Plasma cells are terminally differentiated B cells specialized for antibody secretion. Normal bone remodelling balances osteoclast-mediated resorption with osteoblast-mediated formation, while kidneys filter small proteins and maintain calcium homeostasis.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Multiple myeloma is a clonal plasma-cell neoplasm producing a monoclonal immunoglobulin or light chain. Marrow replacement causes usually normocytic anaemia; myeloma-cell signalling stimulates osteoclasts and suppresses osteoblast activity, causing punched-out osteolytic lesions, fractures and hypercalcaemia. Filtered light chains can injure tubules and form casts, contributing to renal failure; monoclonal light chains can also form AL amyloid. Suppression of normal immunoglobulins predisposes to recurrent infections.

Clonal plasma cells → M-protein/light chains + marrow infiltration + osteoclast activation → CRAB: hyperCalcaemia, Renal injury, Anaemia, Bone lesions

3. Why this answer follows

Myeloma stimulates osteoclast activity while suppressing osteoblast-mediated repair. Bone is therefore removed without an equivalent sclerotic response, producing sharply demarcated lytic ‘punched-out’ lesions rather than osteoblastic lesions. Monoclonal proteins also promote rouleaux, which accelerates ESR markedly.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Osteoblastic why not / analysis
Myeloma is classically lytic rather than osteoblastic because osteoclast activation predominates and osteoblast repair is suppressed.
B. Osteoporotic why not / analysis
Generalized osteoporosis can occur in myeloma, but the classic characteristic lesion is osteolytic/punched-out destruction.
C. Osteolytic correct
Correct. This matches the destructive bone-resorption pattern of multiple myeloma.
D. Osteomalacia why not / analysis
Osteomalacia is defective mineralization, not the focal punched-out destructive process of myeloma.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A lytic skull lesion, very high ESR/rouleaux, M-spike or Bence Jones protein should all converge on the same plasma-cell pathophysiology.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 363–368. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 38
Confirmed
Which of the following finding is pathognomic feature of Mycosis fungoides?
AI
Answer: C — Pautrier microabscesses
Confirmed · Mycosis fungoides & cutaneous T-cell lymphoma

1. Start with the normal physiology

Skin-homing T cells normally traffic between blood, lymphoid tissue and epidermal/dermal compartments as part of immune surveillance.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Mycosis fungoides is a neoplasm of mature helper T cells with progressive skin involvement. Neoplastic lymphocytes show epidermotropism; clusters of atypical cells within the epidermis form Pautrier microabscesses. Sézary syndrome represents a leukemic phase with circulating cerebriform Sézary cells.

Clonal mature T cells → skin homing/epidermotropism → patches/plaques/tumours → Pautrier microabscesses; leukemic spread → Sézary cells

3. Why this answer follows

Pautrier microabscesses are intraepidermal clusters of atypical neoplastic T lymphocytes and reflect the epidermotropism of mycosis fungoides. Sézary cells are circulating cerebriform malignant T cells seen in the leukemic phase/Sézary syndrome; a skin rash alone is nonspecific.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Sezary cells why not / analysis
Sézary cells are circulating cerebriform malignant T cells of Sézary syndrome/leukemic CTCL, not the pathognomonic epidermal structure asked for mycosis fungoides.
B. Starry sky appearance why not / analysis
Starry-sky histology is classically associated with Burkitt lymphoma because macrophages ingest numerous apoptotic tumour cells.
C. Pautrier microabscesses correct
Correct. Pautrier microabscesses are epidermal collections of atypical T cells characteristic of mycosis fungoides.
D. Skin rashes why not / analysis
A rash is clinically nonspecific and cannot serve as a pathognomonic histologic feature.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Pautrier microabscess = mycosis fungoides. Sézary cell = circulating leukemic counterpart/phase.
Source basis: Block 3.7, p. 345. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 39
Confirmed
Lymphocytes constitutes what percentage of the body’s weight of an adult male?
AI
Answer: D — 0.5%
Confirmed · Leukocyte physiology, lymphocyte biology & reactive counts

1. Start with the normal physiology

Neutrophils arise in marrow through myeloblast → promyelocyte → myelocyte → metamyelocyte → band → segmented stages, with a large marrow storage pool allowing rapid release. B cells mature in marrow, T cells in thymus, and antigen-driven immune responses occur in secondary lymphoid tissues such as lymph nodes, spleen and mucosal lymphoid tissue. Mature helper T cells are typically CD4-positive/CD8-negative and coordinate immune responses through cytokines.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Leukocyte counts change through altered production, release, margination, tissue migration or destruction. Reactive lymphocytosis accompanies selected infections such as pertussis, infectious mononucleosis, measles and hepatitis. Immune neutropenia can occur when antibodies or immune dysregulation increase peripheral neutrophil destruction, as in Felty syndrome.

Stimulus/disease → altered marrow production or peripheral distribution/destruction → characteristic leukocyte-count pattern

3. Why this answer follows

Prof Der states lymphocytes account for about 0.5–1% of body weight; 0.5% is the listed best answer. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 45% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. 24% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. 5% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. 0.5% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Differentiate a quantitative disorder (too many/too few cells) from a functional disorder (cells present but defective). Several distractors are neutrophil-function syndromes, not causes of neutropenia.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 211–228. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2022 Parallel · Question 40
Confirmed
Which of the following is the easiest way to diagnose multiple myeloma at the primary health care centre?
AI
Answer: D — Heating urine collection form the patient
Confirmed · Multiple myeloma: plasma-cell clonality, bone disease & paraproteins

1. Start with the normal physiology

Plasma cells are terminally differentiated B cells specialized for antibody secretion. Normal bone remodelling balances osteoclast-mediated resorption with osteoblast-mediated formation, while kidneys filter small proteins and maintain calcium homeostasis.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Multiple myeloma is a clonal plasma-cell neoplasm producing a monoclonal immunoglobulin or light chain. Marrow replacement causes usually normocytic anaemia; myeloma-cell signalling stimulates osteoclasts and suppresses osteoblast activity, causing punched-out osteolytic lesions, fractures and hypercalcaemia. Filtered light chains can injure tubules and form casts, contributing to renal failure; monoclonal light chains can also form AL amyloid. Suppression of normal immunoglobulins predisposes to recurrent infections.

Clonal plasma cells → M-protein/light chains + marrow infiltration + osteoclast activation → CRAB: hyperCalcaemia, Renal injury, Anaemia, Bone lesions

3. Why this answer follows

Historically, Bence Jones light chains can be suggested by characteristic heat precipitation of urine, making it a simple low-resource screening maneuver. Modern diagnosis is not based on this alone: serum/urine electrophoresis with immunofixation, serum free-light-chain testing, marrow assessment and imaging are used. The MCQ is asking for the easiest basic-level test among the listed options.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Electrophoresis why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. Clinical history why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. X-ray of the bone why not / analysis
Bone itself is not the classic target; bone marrow is affected because haematopoietic precursors divide rapidly.
D. Heating urine collection form the patient correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A lytic skull lesion, very high ESR/rouleaux, M-spike or Bence Jones protein should all converge on the same plasma-cell pathophysiology.

2022 Parallel · Question 41
Confirmed
Pel-Ebstein fever is most likely a clinical feature in which of the following haematological malignancy?
AI
Answer: B — Nodular sclerosing HL
Confirmed · Hodgkin lymphoma, EBV risk & classic clinical associations

1. Start with the normal physiology

Germinal-center B cells that fail appropriate selection normally undergo apoptosis. Immune-cell interactions within lymph nodes are tightly regulated by cytokine networks.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Hodgkin lymphoma contains a minority of neoplastic Reed–Sternberg cells within a prominent reactive inflammatory background. In a subset, EBV contributes to oncogenic signalling and survival; prior infectious mononucleosis therefore increases later Hodgkin-lymphoma risk. Clinical disease often presents with painless lymphadenopathy and constitutional ‘B symptoms’; Pel–Ebstein fever is a classic historical association.

Susceptible B cell ± EBV-driven survival signalling → Reed–Sternberg clone → cytokine-rich inflammatory microenvironment → lymphadenopathy/B symptoms

3. Why this answer follows

Pel–Ebstein fever describes a historical pattern of cyclical fever classically associated with Hodgkin lymphoma. It is neither sensitive nor specific enough to diagnose Hodgkin lymphoma, but it remains a classic exam association.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Burkitt’s lymphoma why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
B. Nodular sclerosing HL correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. ALL why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
D. NHL why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 EBV/infectious mononucleosis is an association, not a statement that every Hodgkin lymphoma is EBV-positive.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 356–360. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 42
Confirmed
What histological variant is the most likely?
AI
Answer: C — Endemic
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

A rapidly enlarging mandibular tumour in a child from endemic African settings is the classic epidemiologic phenotype of endemic Burkitt lymphoma. The ‘variant’ is defined by epidemiology/clinical pattern rather than a different microscopic morphology; all forms share the same basic high-grade B-cell histology.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Organ transplanted why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. HIV associated why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. Endemic correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Sporadic why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2022 Parallel · Question 43
Confirmed
The most likely oral presentation will be?
AI
Answer: B — Dental anargic
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

Endemic Burkitt lymphoma can infiltrate jaw bones around developing teeth, causing rapid bony expansion, tooth loosening/displacement, premature eruption and a chaotic dental appearance often described as dental anarchy. These manifestations follow destructive tumour growth within the maxillofacial skeleton.

2022 Parallel · Question 44
Confirmed
The best investigation to confirm the diagnosis is?
AI
Answer: C — Core needle biopsy
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

A lymphoma diagnosis requires actual tumour tissue because architecture, cytology and immunophenotype determine the subtype. CT/MRI define extent and anatomy, and marrow studies assist staging in selected cases, but they cannot replace tissue biopsy for the primary histologic diagnosis.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. CT scan of the head why not / analysis
Imaging demonstrates anatomy and extent but does not provide the tissue architecture/immunophenotype needed for definitive lymphoma classification.
B. MRI of the head why not / analysis
Imaging demonstrates anatomy and extent but does not provide the tissue architecture/immunophenotype needed for definitive lymphoma classification.
C. Core needle biopsy correct
Correct. Tissue sampling provides histology and immunophenotyping and is therefore the confirmatory route for lymphoma.
D. Bone marrow aspirate why not / analysis
Bone itself is not the classic target; bone marrow is affected because haematopoietic precursors divide rapidly.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2022 Parallel · Question 45
Confirmed
What is the cure rate for Burkitt’s lymphoma?
AI
Answer: A — 90%
Confirmed · Burkitt lymphoma: MYC dysregulation, explosive growth & starry sky

1. Start with the normal physiology

MYC is a transcription factor that promotes cell growth, metabolism and cell-cycle entry but is normally tightly regulated. Germinal-center B cells physiologically undergo immunoglobulin gene rearrangement and rapid proliferation.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Burkitt lymphoma classically carries a MYC translocation, most often t(8;14), placing MYC under strong immunoglobulin enhancer control. This drives one of the fastest proliferation rates in human cancer. High proliferation is matched by high apoptosis; macrophages ingest apoptotic debris, producing the ‘starry-sky’ histology. Endemic African Burkitt lymphoma commonly involves the jaw/facial bones and is strongly EBV-associated, while sporadic disease more often presents with an abdominal mass and has a much lower EBV association.

t(8;14) MYC activation → extreme B-cell proliferation + apoptosis → bulky rapidly growing tumour + starry-sky histology

3. Why this answer follows

Burkitt lymphoma is biologically aggressive but highly chemosensitive because of its rapid cell cycling. With intensive combination chemotherapy and appropriate supportive care, cure rates in children can approach about 90% in many settings, which explains the apparently paradoxical combination of explosive growth and high curability.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 90% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. 75% why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
C. 67% why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
D. 60% why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Jaw mass in an African child → endemic Burkitt. Abdominal mass in a Western child/young adult → sporadic Burkitt. MYC is the mechanistic anchor.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 347–351. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 46
Confirmed
The EBV is implicated in what proportion of sporadic Burkitt’s lymphoma?
AI
Answer: A — 25%
Confirmed · Burkitt lymphoma: MYC dysregulation, explosive growth & starry sky

1. Start with the normal physiology

MYC is a transcription factor that promotes cell growth, metabolism and cell-cycle entry but is normally tightly regulated. Germinal-center B cells physiologically undergo immunoglobulin gene rearrangement and rapid proliferation.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Burkitt lymphoma classically carries a MYC translocation, most often t(8;14), placing MYC under strong immunoglobulin enhancer control. This drives one of the fastest proliferation rates in human cancer. High proliferation is matched by high apoptosis; macrophages ingest apoptotic debris, producing the ‘starry-sky’ histology. Endemic African Burkitt lymphoma commonly involves the jaw/facial bones and is strongly EBV-associated, while sporadic disease more often presents with an abdominal mass and has a much lower EBV association.

t(8;14) MYC activation → extreme B-cell proliferation + apoptosis → bulky rapidly growing tumour + starry-sky histology

3. Why this answer follows

EBV is present in nearly all endemic Burkitt lymphomas but only a minority of sporadic cases. Prof. Der's slide states less than 30% for sporadic disease, so 25% is the best numeric option when offered. MYC dysregulation remains the central oncogenic event in both forms.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 25% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. 30% why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
C. 45% why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
D. 35% why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Jaw mass in an African child → endemic Burkitt. Abdominal mass in a Western child/young adult → sporadic Burkitt. MYC is the mechanistic anchor.

2022 Parallel · Question 47
Confirmed + note
What id the probability that Mr. Kofi would have haemophilia A?
AI
Answer: C — 1 (100%).
Confirmed + note · Haemophilia physiology, genetics & factor assays

1. Start with the normal physiology

Factor VIII acts as a cofactor with activated factor IX on phospholipid surfaces to accelerate factor X activation in the intrinsic tenase complex. This amplification is important for generating the thrombin burst needed for a stable fibrin clot. vWF stabilizes circulating factor VIII.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Haemophilia A is factor VIII deficiency and haemophilia B is factor IX deficiency; both are usually X-linked recessive and clinically produce deep bleeding, large haematomas and haemarthroses rather than petechiae. Screening shows prolonged aPTT with normal PT, but definitive diagnosis and distinction between A and B require specific factor assays. Mild haemophilia A can respond to desmopressin, which raises endogenous vWF and factor VIII.

↓VIII or ↓IX → impaired intrinsic tenase → inadequate factor X/thrombin burst → weak fibrin clot → delayed/deep bleeding and haemarthroses

3. Why this answer follows

If Mr Dery/Kofi’s mother truly had haemophilia A as an affected female, she would transmit the affected X chromosome to every son. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. ½ why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. ¼ why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. 1 correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. 1/8 why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 aPTT tells you the pathway is abnormal; the factor assay tells you which disease. Do not confuse a screening test with a definitive diagnosis.

6. Question-quality note

If Mr Dery/Kofi’s mother truly had haemophilia A as an affected female, she would transmit the affected X chromosome to every son.

Source basis: X-linked recessive inheritance. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 48
Confirmed + note
What percentage of Mr. Kofi’s sisters had clinical disease?
AI
Answer: D — None of the above (0% clinically affected sisters, under the usual assumptions).
Confirmed + note · X-linked recessive inheritance & haemophilia pedigrees

1. Start with the normal physiology

Males have one X chromosome, so a pathogenic recessive allele on that X is expressed because there is no second normal copy. Females usually require two pathogenic alleles to be fully affected, while heterozygous females are typically carriers, though skewed X-inactivation can modify expression.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

For X-linked recessive disease, affected fathers transmit their mutant X to all daughters and to no sons. Carrier mothers transmit the mutant X to 50% of sons (who become affected) and 50% of daughters (who become carriers). Probability questions must be solved generation by generation; never multiply percentages until you have first established which person is an obligate carrier.

Identify genotype of ancestor → determine obligate carriers → trace maternal X transmission → condition on child sex → calculate final probability

3. Why this answer follows

An affected mother crossed with a normal father produces carrier daughters, who are usually clinically unaffected. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 50% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. 25% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. 75% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. None of the above correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Draw the pedigree. Fathers do not transmit X-linked disease to sons. This single rule eliminates many distractors.

6. Question-quality note

An affected mother crossed with a normal father produces carrier daughters, who are usually clinically unaffected.

Source basis: X-linked recessive inheritance. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You

Confirmed + note · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

An affected father and an obligate-carrier mother produce an affected child in 50% of pregnancies overall. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 50% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. 25% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. 75% why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. None of the above why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.

6. Question-quality note

An affected father and an obligate-carrier mother produce an affected child in 50% of pregnancies overall.

2022 Parallel · Question 50
Confirmed + note
Karyotyping of Edmund mother’s chromosome revealed trisomy 21 and no other abnormality. What is the probability that Edmund’s sons will have the disease?
AI
Answer: D — None of the above (0%).
Confirmed + note · X-linked recessive inheritance & haemophilia pedigrees

1. Start with the normal physiology

Males have one X chromosome, so a pathogenic recessive allele on that X is expressed because there is no second normal copy. Females usually require two pathogenic alleles to be fully affected, while heterozygous females are typically carriers, though skewed X-inactivation can modify expression.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

For X-linked recessive disease, affected fathers transmit their mutant X to all daughters and to no sons. Carrier mothers transmit the mutant X to 50% of sons (who become affected) and 50% of daughters (who become carriers). Probability questions must be solved generation by generation; never multiply percentages until you have first established which person is an obligate carrier.

Identify genotype of ancestor → determine obligate carriers → trace maternal X transmission → condition on child sex → calculate final probability

3. Why this answer follows

A father gives his Y chromosome, not his X chromosome, to his sons; therefore Edmund cannot transmit an X-linked haemophilia allele to sons. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. ½ why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. ¼ why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. 1/8 why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. None of the above correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Draw the pedigree. Fathers do not transmit X-linked disease to sons. This single rule eliminates many distractors.

6. Question-quality note

A father gives his Y chromosome, not his X chromosome, to his sons; therefore Edmund cannot transmit an X-linked haemophilia allele to sons.

Source basis: X-linked recessive inheritance. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2022 Parallel · Question 51
Ambiguous item
What is the probability that Edmund’s daughter will be normal?
AI
Answer: A — ½ (50%) if “normal” means non-carrier.
Ambiguous item · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

Christiana is an obligate carrier if her father is affected. With a normal male partner, half of daughters are non-carriers and half carriers; all are clinically unaffected in the usual model. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

2022 Parallel · Question 52
Confirmed
How is Haemophilia A diagnosed?
AI
Answer: C — Factor assay
Confirmed · Haemophilia physiology, genetics & factor assays

1. Start with the normal physiology

Factor VIII acts as a cofactor with activated factor IX on phospholipid surfaces to accelerate factor X activation in the intrinsic tenase complex. This amplification is important for generating the thrombin burst needed for a stable fibrin clot. vWF stabilizes circulating factor VIII.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Haemophilia A is factor VIII deficiency and haemophilia B is factor IX deficiency; both are usually X-linked recessive and clinically produce deep bleeding, large haematomas and haemarthroses rather than petechiae. Screening shows prolonged aPTT with normal PT, but definitive diagnosis and distinction between A and B require specific factor assays. Mild haemophilia A can respond to desmopressin, which raises endogenous vWF and factor VIII.

↓VIII or ↓IX → impaired intrinsic tenase → inadequate factor X/thrombin burst → weak fibrin clot → delayed/deep bleeding and haemarthroses

3. Why this answer follows

Screening coagulation tests identify which part of haemostasis is abnormal, but definitive diagnosis requires measurement of the relevant factor activity. Haemophilia A and B can look identical clinically and both prolong aPTT; only factor VIII versus IX assays distinguish them reliably.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. PTT why not / analysis
aPTT screens the intrinsic/common pathways and is prolonged in factor VIII or IX deficiency, but it does not by itself distinguish haemophilia A from B.
B. PT why not / analysis
PT evaluates the extrinsic/common pathway and is usually normal in isolated haemophilia A/B.
C. Factor assay correct
Correct. Specific factor activity measurement is the definitive test that distinguishes factor VIII from factor IX deficiency.
D. Clinical history why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 aPTT tells you the pathway is abnormal; the factor assay tells you which disease. Do not confuse a screening test with a definitive diagnosis.
Source basis: Pathology Secrets / haemostasis principles. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 53
Confirmed + note
Which of the following viruses is implicated in the aetiology of aplastic anaemia in human?
AI
Answer: A — HIV.
Confirmed + note · Aplastic anaemia & marrow-failure physiology

1. Start with the normal physiology

A healthy marrow maintains all three major blood-cell lineages through viable stem cells supported by a functioning stromal niche and growth-factor signals.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Aplastic anaemia is failure of multipotent haematopoietic stem/progenitor cells, often immune-mediated, producing hypocellular fatty marrow and pancytopenia. Because production fails, reticulocytes are low. Causes include drugs, chemicals, radiation, immune mechanisms and selected viral infections. Parvovirus B19 preferentially targets erythroid precursors and classically causes pure red-cell aplasia or aplastic crisis rather than typical trilineage aplastic anaemia.

Stem-cell injury/suppression → hypocellular marrow → ↓RBC + ↓WBC + ↓platelets → anaemia + infection + bleeding

3. Why this answer follows

Aplastic anaemia is trilineage marrow failure, and viral associations depend on the exact syndrome. HIV has been linked to acquired marrow failure in broad teaching lists. Parvovirus B19 has a strong tropism for erythroid precursors and is classically associated with transient aplastic crisis in chronic haemolytic anaemia or pure red-cell aplasia, not the typical pancytopenic aplastic-anaemia pattern.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. HIV correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. HBV why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. Parvovirus B19 why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. HPV why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 The key discriminator is trilineage production failure with low reticulocytes; do not confuse this with isolated erythroid suppression.

6. Question-quality note

Pathology Secrets lists infections including HIV and EBV among acquired causes of aplastic anaemia. Parvovirus B19 is classically associated with pure red-cell aplasia/aplastic crisis rather than typical pancytopenic aplastic anaemia.

Source basis: Pathology Secrets, Ch. 9 (Aplastic anemia causes). The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 54
Multiple correct
Which of the following defines sickle cell disease?
AI
Answer: Multiple — B, C and D — SS, SC and Sβ-thalassaemia are all sickle cell disease.
Multiple correct · Sickle-cell molecular physiology & vaso-occlusion

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal HbA remains soluble during ordinary cycles of oxygen loading and unloading, while RBC membrane flexibility permits passage through microvessels. HbF inhibits HbS polymerization, which is why newborns are relatively protected early in life.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

HbS results from a β-globin point mutation that substitutes valine for glutamate. When deoxygenated, HbS molecules polymerize into long fibers, distorting RBCs. Repeated sickling damages the membrane, causing irreversible rigidity and haemolysis. Sickled cells also obstruct microcirculation, causing ischemia and infarction. Hypoxia, acidosis, dehydration and slow flow promote polymerization. Sickle-cell disease includes genotypes with two abnormal β-globin alleles and at least one HbS allele, such as HbSS, HbSC and HbS/β-thalassaemia—not simple trait HbAS.

β-globin mutation → deoxy-HbS polymerization → sickling → membrane damage + rigidity → haemolysis AND microvascular obstruction → ischemic organ injury

3. Why this answer follows

Sickle-cell disease is not synonymous with HbSS alone. The pathophysiologic requirement is inheritance of two abnormal β-globin alleles with at least one HbS allele sufficient to cause clinically important sickling—classically HbSS, HbSC and HbS/β-thalassaemia. HbAS is sickle trait and usually does not produce the full disease phenotype.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Genotype AS why not / analysis
HbAS is sickle trait: one normal βA allele substantially limits polymerization under ordinary conditions, so it is not the full sickle-cell disease phenotype.
B. Genotype SS why not / analysis
HbSS is classic sickle-cell anaemia and is a sickle-cell disease genotype.
C. Genotype SC why not / analysis
HbSC is a compound heterozygous sickle-cell disease genotype, often clinically milder than HbSS but still disease.
D. Genotype SB-thal why not / analysis
HbS/β-thalassaemia is a sickle-cell disease genotype because HbS is paired with reduced/absent βA production.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Separate “trait” from “disease,” and separate the two major mechanisms: chronic haemolysis and episodic vaso-occlusion.

6. Question-quality note

AS is sickle trait. Prof Der defines sickle cell disease as inheritance of two abnormal haemoglobin genes, at least one being a sickling gene.

Source basis: Block 3.7, p. 131. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 55
Confirmed
The protein from food which first binds and transport vitamin B12 in the upper gastrointestinal tract is?
AI
Answer: D — Cobalaphilines
Confirmed · Vitamin B12 physiology & megaloblastic pathology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Food-bound cobalamin is released in the stomach by acid and pepsin, initially binds salivary/gastric R-binders (cobalophilins/haptocorrin), is freed by pancreatic proteases in the duodenum, then binds intrinsic factor from gastric parietal cells. The intrinsic-factor–B12 complex is absorbed in the terminal ileum and B12 circulates bound mainly to transcobalamin II.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

B12 deficiency impairs methionine synthase activity and traps folate as methyl-THF, producing functionally inadequate tetrahydrofolate for DNA synthesis. Rapidly dividing tissues develop nuclear–cytoplasmic asynchrony: cytoplasm matures while nuclear DNA replication lags. This causes megaloblastic marrow change, macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils and ineffective intramedullary haematopoiesis. B12 deficiency also causes neurologic injury through disordered myelin metabolism—an important distinction from isolated folate deficiency.

Impaired B12 availability → defective DNA synthesis + abnormal myelin metabolism → megaloblastosis/ineffective erythropoiesis + neurologic disease

3. Why this answer follows

Immediately after food B12 is released in the stomach, it binds cobalophilin/haptocorrin (R-binder), which protects it from gastric acidity. In the duodenum pancreatic enzymes digest the R-binder; B12 then transfers to intrinsic factor for terminal-ileal uptake. Therefore ‘first binds’ and ‘protein necessary for absorption’ are different questions.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Transcobalamines why not / analysis
Transcobalamin transports B12 in plasma after intestinal uptake; it is not the first gastric binder and is not intrinsic factor.
B. Intrinsic factor why not / analysis
Intrinsic factor is made by gastric parietal cells and is required for terminal-ileal B12 uptake, but it is not the first binder of food-released B12.
C. Cobalamines why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. Cobalaphilines correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Follow B12 through the GI tract step by step. A question may target the first binder, intrinsic factor source, terminal ileal absorption, pernicious anaemia or neurologic consequences.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 81–84. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 56
Confirmed
Oral iron absorption maximally occurs in the?
AI
Answer: C — First part of the duodenum
Confirmed · Iron physiology, absorption & iron-deficiency anaemia

1. Start with the normal physiology

Iron is essential for haem synthesis. Dietary non-haem iron is best absorbed in the duodenum/proximal small bowel after reduction to the ferrous form; haem iron is absorbed more efficiently. Enterocyte iron can be stored in ferritin or exported through ferroportin to plasma transferrin. Hepcidin from the liver is the master negative regulator: it binds ferroportin and promotes its internalization, reducing intestinal absorption and macrophage iron release.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Iron deficiency first depletes stores (falling ferritin), then reduces circulating available iron and transferrin saturation, and finally limits haemoglobin synthesis. Erythroblasts undergo extra divisions while trying to reach an adequate intracellular haemoglobin concentration, producing microcytic, hypochromic cells. Chronic blood loss—especially gastrointestinal blood loss in older adults—is a classic mechanism.

↓Iron stores → ↓haem synthesis → delayed haemoglobinization → extra erythroblast divisions → microcytosis + hypochromia → reduced O₂ carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

The duodenum is optimized for iron uptake through apical transport systems and regulation by systemic iron need. As luminal contents pass distally, iron availability and uptake decline. Therefore proximal duodenum is the exam answer even though some absorption also occurs in proximal jejunum.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Stomach why not / analysis
The stomach prepares nutrients and secretes intrinsic factor, but it is not the principal site of iron or B12 absorption asked here.
B. Jejunum why not / analysis
The jejunum is important for folate absorption and can absorb some iron, but the duodenum is the principal/maximal iron-absorption site.
C. First part of the duodenum correct
Correct. The proximal duodenum is the principal site of iron absorption.
D. Ileum why not / analysis
The terminal ileum is the key site for intrinsic-factor–B12 absorption, not the main site of iron absorption.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Know both the anatomy (duodenum) and the regulatory physiology (hepcidin–ferroportin). “Iron absorbed where?” and “why microcytic?” are two faces of the same pathway.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 71–74. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 57
Confirmed
Which of the following red blood cell indices is commonly used in morphological classification of anaemia in humans?
AI
Answer: D — MCV
Confirmed · Erythropoiesis, oxygen sensing & red-cell indices

1. Start with the normal physiology

Red-cell production is regulated primarily by tissue oxygen delivery. Renal peritubular interstitial cells sense hypoxia through oxygen-dependent HIF signalling; hypoxia stabilizes HIF, increasing erythropoietin (EPO). EPO supports survival and proliferation of late erythroid progenitors, increasing reticulocyte output. A mature RBC is a deformable biconcave disc packed with haemoglobin and normally survives about 120 days.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Anaemia lowers oxygen delivery, so a healthy marrow should increase reticulocyte production. A high corrected reticulocyte count therefore points toward peripheral loss/destruction with preserved marrow reserve; a low corrected count points toward impaired production. Morphologic indices answer a different question: MCV classifies cell size, MCH/MCHC reflect haemoglobinization, and the haematocrit estimates packed red-cell volume.

↓O₂ delivery → renal hypoxia sensing/HIF → ↑EPO → erythroid progenitor survival → ↑reticulocytes → restoration of oxygen-carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

MCV directly reports average RBC volume and is the backbone of morphological classification: microcytic, normocytic or macrocytic. MCH and MCHC describe haemoglobin content/concentration, while Hb measures anaemia severity rather than cell size. This distinction separates ‘how anaemic?’ from ‘what morphology?’

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Hb why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. MCH why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. MCHC why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. MCV correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Do not interpret a raw reticulocyte percentage in anaemia without correcting it for the reduced red-cell mass. Separate “production response” from “cell morphology.”
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 58
Confirmed
The following are cofactors in erythropoiesis except?
AI
Answer: D — Calcium
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

Prof. Der separates essential nutritional factors (iron, B12, folate) from a list of cofactors such as manganese, cobalt and several vitamins. Old questions sometimes mix these categories. Calcium is not part of the listed erythropoietic/haemoglobin-synthesis cofactor set; zinc is also not on the specific slide list.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Folate why not / analysis
Folate is an essential nutritional factor for DNA synthesis; in Prof. Der's slide taxonomy it is listed separately from the ‘cofactor’ group.
B. Manganese why not / analysis
This appears on Prof. Der's listed haemoglobin/erythropoietic cofactor set.
C. Cobalt why not / analysis
This appears on Prof. Der's listed haemoglobin/erythropoietic cofactor set.
D. Calcium correct
Correct. Calcium is crucial in coagulation and many cellular processes, but it is not on Prof. Der's listed erythropoietic/haemoglobin-synthesis cofactor set in this context.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 59
Confirmed
In a 72.0 kg male, what percentage of haemopoiesis activity takes place in the bone marrow?
AI
Answer: D — 100%
Confirmed · Haematopoiesis & marrow physiology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal blood-cell production begins with a rare self-renewing CD34-positive pluripotent haematopoietic stem cell in the marrow. Stem cells balance self-renewal with commitment into myeloid and lymphoid progenitors. The marrow stromal microenvironment, adhesion molecules, cytokines and lineage-specific growth factors regulate survival, proliferation and differentiation. In fetal life the dominant sites shift from yolk sac to liver/spleen and finally bone marrow; in adults active marrow is concentrated in the axial skeleton and proximal long bones.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Disease appears when one of four things goes wrong: the stem/progenitor compartment is quantitatively lost, maturation is blocked, proliferation becomes autonomous, or mature cells are destroyed faster than the marrow can replace them. The peripheral blood count is therefore the end result of marrow production, release, distribution, sequestration and destruction—not simply a measure of how many cells the marrow is making.

Stem cell → committed progenitor → growth-factor signalling → maturation → marrow release → peripheral survival/distribution

3. Why this answer follows

Normal adult haematopoiesis occurs in bone marrow; extramedullary sites are abnormal/reactive in adults. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 25% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. 50% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. 75% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. 100% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Prof. Der often asks the sequence, site, growth factor or developmental stage rather than naming the process directly. Always locate the question on this production pathway.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 60
Confirmed
Which of the following growth factors act on pluripotent cells?
AI
Answer: C — SCF
Confirmed · Haematopoiesis & marrow physiology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal blood-cell production begins with a rare self-renewing CD34-positive pluripotent haematopoietic stem cell in the marrow. Stem cells balance self-renewal with commitment into myeloid and lymphoid progenitors. The marrow stromal microenvironment, adhesion molecules, cytokines and lineage-specific growth factors regulate survival, proliferation and differentiation. In fetal life the dominant sites shift from yolk sac to liver/spleen and finally bone marrow; in adults active marrow is concentrated in the axial skeleton and proximal long bones.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Disease appears when one of four things goes wrong: the stem/progenitor compartment is quantitatively lost, maturation is blocked, proliferation becomes autonomous, or mature cells are destroyed faster than the marrow can replace them. The peripheral blood count is therefore the end result of marrow production, release, distribution, sequestration and destruction—not simply a measure of how many cells the marrow is making.

Stem cell → committed progenitor → growth-factor signalling → maturation → marrow release → peripheral survival/distribution

3. Why this answer follows

Stem cell factor binds c-KIT on early haematopoietic stem/progenitor cells and supports survival and proliferation. In Prof. Der's hierarchy, IL-1/TNF act mainly on stromal cells, while IL-3/IL-6/GM-CSF act on early multipotential progenitors and lineage-specific factors act later.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. IL-1 why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. TNF why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. SCF correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. IL-3 why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Prof. Der often asks the sequence, site, growth factor or developmental stage rather than naming the process directly. Always locate the question on this production pathway.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 3–17. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 61
Confirmed
Weibel-Palade bodies are synthesized in which of the following cells?
AI
Answer: D — Endothelial cells
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Weibel–Palade bodies are specialized endothelial storage granules, classically containing vWF and P-selectin. Rapid release of vWF after endothelial stimulation supports platelet adhesion at sites of vascular injury. Platelets contain alpha and dense granules, not Weibel–Palade bodies.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Magakaryocytes why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. Platelets why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. Liver cells why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.
D. Endothelial cells correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
Source basis: Pathology Secrets / haemostasis principles. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 62
Confirmed
What is the normal life span of platelets in peripheral blood?
AI
Answer: C — 9-10 days
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Platelets are anucleate fragments of megakaryocyte cytoplasm that circulate for roughly 7–10 days before senescence and clearance, mainly by the spleen. Thrombopoietin regulates production largely by how much free hormone remains after binding platelets/megakaryocytes.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 100-120 days why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. 90-100 days why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. 9-10 days correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. 1-5 days why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
