2022 Parallel · Question 63
Confirmed
The picture shows which type of acute leukaemia in the FAB stage?
AI
Answer: A — ALL2
Confirmed · Acute leukaemia: clonal biology, marrow failure & blast thresholds

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal haematopoiesis requires progenitor proliferation to remain coupled to orderly differentiation and apoptosis. Mature cells leave marrow while immature blasts remain a small minority.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Acute leukaemia arises when acquired genetic lesions give a clone increased proliferation/survival and—critically—a block in differentiation. Blasts accumulate in marrow, replacing normal haematopoiesis. Anaemia causes fatigue, neutropenia causes infection, thrombocytopenia causes bleeding, and high circulating blast counts can cause leukostasis. Prof. Der’s current slides use >20% blasts in blood or marrow as the general diagnostic threshold, with genetically defined exceptions; the older FAB system used 30%.

Driver mutations → ↑proliferation + ↓apoptosis + maturation arrest → blast accumulation → marrow replacement → cytopenias ± leukostasis/infiltration

3. Why this answer follows

The marrow image and the linked follow-up items fit FAB ALL-L2. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. ALL2 correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. AML1 why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
C. AML2 why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
D. AML3 why not / analysis
FAB M3 is acute promyelocytic leukaemia, classically associated with t(15;17) and DIC.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 A high total WBC does not mean effective immunity: the cells may be malignant blasts. Also watch for old 30% FAB-threshold questions that conflict with current >20% criteria.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You

2022 Parallel · Question 65
Confirmed
The common cell types in this condition is?
AI
Answer: B — T lymphocytes
Confirmed · Leukocyte physiology, lymphocyte biology & reactive counts

1. Start with the normal physiology

Neutrophils arise in marrow through myeloblast → promyelocyte → myelocyte → metamyelocyte → band → segmented stages, with a large marrow storage pool allowing rapid release. B cells mature in marrow, T cells in thymus, and antigen-driven immune responses occur in secondary lymphoid tissues such as lymph nodes, spleen and mucosal lymphoid tissue. Mature helper T cells are typically CD4-positive/CD8-negative and coordinate immune responses through cytokines.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Leukocyte counts change through altered production, release, margination, tissue migration or destruction. Reactive lymphocytosis accompanies selected infections such as pertussis, infectious mononucleosis, measles and hepatitis. Immune neutropenia can occur when antibodies or immune dysregulation increase peripheral neutrophil destruction, as in Felty syndrome.

Stimulus/disease → altered marrow production or peripheral distribution/destruction → characteristic leukocyte-count pattern

3. Why this answer follows

Prof Der notes that most ALL-L2 cases are T-cell type. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. B lymphocytes why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. T lymphocytes correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. Marginal cells why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. Mantle cells why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Differentiate a quantitative disorder (too many/too few cells) from a functional disorder (cells present but defective). Several distractors are neutrophil-function syndromes, not causes of neutropenia.

2022 Parallel · Question 69
Confirmed
The most appropriate test to request for this patient is?
AI
Answer: C — Bleeding time
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Among the listed tests, bleeding time assesses primary haemostasis/platelet function; the CBC already establishes the platelet-count abnormality. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. PTT why not / analysis
aPTT screens the intrinsic/common pathways and is prolonged in factor VIII or IX deficiency, but it does not by itself distinguish haemophilia A from B.
B. PT why not / analysis
PT evaluates the extrinsic/common pathway and is usually normal in isolated haemophilia A/B.
C. Bleeding time correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Factor assay why not / analysis
Specific factor activity measurement is the definitive test that distinguishes factor VIII from factor IX deficiency.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 70
Confirmed
Blood and or products given to congestive heart failure due to severe anemia is?
AI
Answer: B — Packed red blood cells
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

Packed RBCs deliver haemoglobin with substantially less plasma volume than whole blood. In heart failure, minimizing volume is crucial because excess intravascular expansion can precipitate pulmonary oedema. Transfusion should still be cautious and clinically justified.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Whole blood why not / analysis
Whole blood adds unnecessary plasma volume when the goal is primarily to raise oxygen-carrying capacity, especially risky in heart failure.
B. Packed red blood cells correct
Correct. Packed RBCs efficiently raise haemoglobin with less volume than whole blood.
C. FFP why not / analysis
Fresh frozen plasma replaces multiple soluble coagulation factors; it does not provide meaningful red-cell mass for anaemia correction.
D. Platelet concentrate why not / analysis
Platelets treat thrombocytopenia/platelet dysfunction, not isolated red-cell anaemia.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 71
Confirmed
Hereditary causes of haemolytic anaemia include except?
AI
Answer: D — Autograft rejection
Confirmed · Haemolysis, splenic physiology & red-cell membrane disease

1. Start with the normal physiology

RBCs must repeatedly deform through narrow splenic cords and sinusoids. Normal membrane–cytoskeleton architecture preserves the biconcave shape, surface-area-to-volume ratio and flexibility needed for this passage. Senescent or abnormal cells are removed mainly by splenic and hepatic macrophages, while haem is converted to bilirubin and iron is recycled.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Extravascular haemolysis occurs when RBCs become poorly deformable or antibody-coated and are trapped and phagocytosed in spleen/liver/marrow. The result is anaemia, reticulocytosis, unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, jaundice and often splenomegaly. In hereditary spherocytosis, defects in vertical interactions between membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer cause progressive membrane loss; cells become spherical, lose deformability and are selectively destroyed in the spleen. Chronic bilirubin overproduction predisposes to pigment gallstones.

Membrane/shape abnormality → ↓deformability → splenic trapping → macrophage phagocytosis → extravascular haemolysis → anaemia + jaundice + splenomegaly + pigment stones

3. Why this answer follows

Intrinsic hereditary RBC disorders include membrane defects, enzyme deficiencies and haemoglobinopathies. Autograft rejection is an immune/transplant phenomenon and is not an inherited intrinsic RBC defect.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Pyruvate kinase deficiency why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. HbS why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. HbC why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
D. Autograft rejection correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Distinguish site of destruction from cause. “Spleen” is not merely enlarged—it is the mechanical filter that exposes poorly deformable cells.
Source basis: Block 3.7, haemolytic anaemia section. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 72
Confirmed
The following causes of anaemia are due to membrane defects
AI
Answer: B — Elliptocytosis
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

Hereditary elliptocytosis is caused by membrane-skeletal defects—often horizontal interactions involving spectrin self-association or protein 4.1—producing elliptical cells. HbS/HbC are haemoglobin structural disorders and G6PD deficiency is an enzymopathy.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. HbS why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. Elliptocytosis correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. HbC why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
D. G6PD deficiency why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

2022 Parallel · Question 73
Confirmed
Which of the following factors is an enzyme?
AI
Answer: C — Thrombin
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Most coagulation factors circulate as inactive zymogens or cofactors. Thrombin is the active serine protease generated from prothrombin; it cleaves fibrinogen to fibrin, activates platelets and amplifies several coagulation factors.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Prothrombin why not / analysis
Prothrombin is the inactive precursor (factor II); activated thrombin is the enzyme that cleaves fibrinogen.
B. Fibrinogen why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. Thrombin correct
Correct. Thrombin is the active protease that converts fibrinogen to fibrin and amplifies coagulation.
D. Factor X why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 74
Confirmed
Factor X is activated by the following except?
AI
Answer: B — V
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Factor X activation occurs through intrinsic tenase (IXa + VIIIa + phospholipid + Ca2+) or extrinsic tenase (tissue factor + VIIa). Factor V is not part of factor-X activation; activated V instead serves as a cofactor with Xa in the prothrombinase complex downstream.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. VIII why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. V correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. Calcium why not / analysis
Calcium is crucial in coagulation and many cellular processes, but it is not on Prof. Der's listed erythropoietic/haemoglobin-synthesis cofactor set in this context.
D. PF3/PL why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
Source basis: Pathology Secrets / haemostasis principles. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2022 Parallel · Question 75
Confirmed
Factor XII is activated by
AI
Answer: E — None of the above
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Factor XII is activated by contact with negatively charged surfaces in the classic intrinsic/contact pathway, along with high-molecular-weight kininogen and prekallikrein. None of factors V, III, II or VIII is the physiologic activator listed in this type of MCQ.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. V why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. III why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. II why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
D. VIII why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
E. None of the above correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

2022 Parallel · Question 76
Confirmed
The following coagulation factors are neutralized by serine protease except?
AI
Answer: C — Factor VIII
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Antithrombin is a serine-protease inhibitor that neutralizes activated serine proteases such as thrombin (IIa), IXa, Xa, XIa and XIIa. Factor VIII is a nonenzymatic cofactor rather than a serine protease, so it is not neutralized by antithrombin in the same way.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. XII why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. II why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. VIII correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. IX why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

2022 Parallel · Question 77
Confirmed + note
How much blood product should be given to raise the Hb to 12 g/dl
AI
Answer: C — 3 units of packed red cells (roughly 750–900 mL, depending on unit volume).
Confirmed + note · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

One unit of packed RBCs typically raises haemoglobin by about 1 g/dL in an average adult; 9 → 12 g/dL therefore needs about 3 units. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 450 ml why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
B. 900 ml why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
C. 3 units correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. 4 units why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.

6. Question-quality note

One unit of packed RBCs typically raises haemoglobin by about 1 g/dL in an average adult; 9 → 12 g/dL therefore needs about 3 units.

2022 Parallel · Question 78
Confirmed
What type of blood products should be given?
AI
Answer: C — Packed red blood cells
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

The correct option best matches the normal physiology and the disease mechanism being tested; the remaining options either belong to a different pathway, describe a downstream association incorrectly, or do not fit the stem's biologic context.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Whole blood why not / analysis
Whole blood adds unnecessary plasma volume when the goal is primarily to raise oxygen-carrying capacity, especially risky in heart failure.
B. Platelet concentrate why not / analysis
Platelets treat thrombocytopenia/platelet dysfunction, not isolated red-cell anaemia.
C. Packed red blood cells correct
Correct. Packed RBCs efficiently raise haemoglobin with less volume than whole blood.
D. Cryoprecipitate why not / analysis
Cryoprecipitate supplies fibrinogen, factor VIII, vWF and factor XIII, not red-cell mass.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

2022 Parallel · Question 79
Confirmed
What is the Haematocrit?
AI
Answer: B — 27%
Confirmed · Erythropoiesis, oxygen sensing & red-cell indices

1. Start with the normal physiology

Red-cell production is regulated primarily by tissue oxygen delivery. Renal peritubular interstitial cells sense hypoxia through oxygen-dependent HIF signalling; hypoxia stabilizes HIF, increasing erythropoietin (EPO). EPO supports survival and proliferation of late erythroid progenitors, increasing reticulocyte output. A mature RBC is a deformable biconcave disc packed with haemoglobin and normally survives about 120 days.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Anaemia lowers oxygen delivery, so a healthy marrow should increase reticulocyte production. A high corrected reticulocyte count therefore points toward peripheral loss/destruction with preserved marrow reserve; a low corrected count points toward impaired production. Morphologic indices answer a different question: MCV classifies cell size, MCH/MCHC reflect haemoglobinization, and the haematocrit estimates packed red-cell volume.

↓O₂ delivery → renal hypoxia sensing/HIF → ↑EPO → erythroid progenitor survival → ↑reticulocytes → restoration of oxygen-carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

A useful bedside approximation is Hct ≈ 3 × Hb when RBC morphology is reasonably normal. Thus Hb 9 g/dL corresponds to an Hct around 27%. This is an estimate and becomes less reliable with marked microcytosis, macrocytosis or abnormal plasma volume.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 12% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. 27% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
C. 36% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. 3% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Do not interpret a raw reticulocyte percentage in anaemia without correcting it for the reduced red-cell mass. Separate “production response” from “cell morphology.”
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 43, 49–50. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 80
Confirmed
What is the corrected reticulocyte count?
AI
Answer: A — 12%
Confirmed · Erythropoiesis, oxygen sensing & red-cell indices

1. Start with the normal physiology

Red-cell production is regulated primarily by tissue oxygen delivery. Renal peritubular interstitial cells sense hypoxia through oxygen-dependent HIF signalling; hypoxia stabilizes HIF, increasing erythropoietin (EPO). EPO supports survival and proliferation of late erythroid progenitors, increasing reticulocyte output. A mature RBC is a deformable biconcave disc packed with haemoglobin and normally survives about 120 days.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Anaemia lowers oxygen delivery, so a healthy marrow should increase reticulocyte production. A high corrected reticulocyte count therefore points toward peripheral loss/destruction with preserved marrow reserve; a low corrected count points toward impaired production. Morphologic indices answer a different question: MCV classifies cell size, MCH/MCHC reflect haemoglobinization, and the haematocrit estimates packed red-cell volume.

↓O₂ delivery → renal hypoxia sensing/HIF → ↑EPO → erythroid progenitor survival → ↑reticulocytes → restoration of oxygen-carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

The raw reticulocyte percentage is artificially inflated when the denominator—circulating RBC mass—is reduced. Corrected reticulocyte % = observed retic % × patient Hct/normal Hct. With Hct 27% and retics 20%, the corrected value is about 12%, indicating a very strong marrow response and therefore peripheral loss/destruction rather than production failure.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. 12% correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. 27% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. 36% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. 3% why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Do not interpret a raw reticulocyte percentage in anaemia without correcting it for the reduced red-cell mass. Separate “production response” from “cell morphology.”
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 43, 49–50. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

AI
Answer: No valid option — No listed option fits. A corrected reticulocyte count of about 12% indicates a strong marrow response, classically haemolysis or acute blood loss.
Outdated / corrected · Erythropoiesis, oxygen sensing & red-cell indices

1. Start with the normal physiology

Red-cell production is regulated primarily by tissue oxygen delivery. Renal peritubular interstitial cells sense hypoxia through oxygen-dependent HIF signalling; hypoxia stabilizes HIF, increasing erythropoietin (EPO). EPO supports survival and proliferation of late erythroid progenitors, increasing reticulocyte output. A mature RBC is a deformable biconcave disc packed with haemoglobin and normally survives about 120 days.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Anaemia lowers oxygen delivery, so a healthy marrow should increase reticulocyte production. A high corrected reticulocyte count therefore points toward peripheral loss/destruction with preserved marrow reserve; a low corrected count points toward impaired production. Morphologic indices answer a different question: MCV classifies cell size, MCH/MCHC reflect haemoglobinization, and the haematocrit estimates packed red-cell volume.

↓O₂ delivery → renal hypoxia sensing/HIF → ↑EPO → erythroid progenitor survival → ↑reticulocytes → restoration of oxygen-carrying capacity

3. Why this answer follows

The raw reticulocyte percentage is artificially inflated when the denominator—circulating RBC mass—is reduced. Corrected reticulocyte % = observed retic % × patient Hct/normal Hct. With Hct 27% and retics 20%, the corrected value is about 12%, indicating a very strong marrow response and therefore peripheral loss/destruction rather than production failure.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Aplastic anaemia why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
B. Sepsis why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Do not interpret a raw reticulocyte percentage in anaemia without correcting it for the reduced red-cell mass. Separate “production response” from “cell morphology.”

6. Question-quality note

Aplastic anaemia, sepsis-related marrow suppression and many malignancy-associated anaemias do not explain such a high corrected reticulocyte response.

2022 Parallel · Question 82
Confirmed
Which step of haemostasis is affected in Glanzmann’s disease?
AI
Answer: C — Platelet plug formation
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Glanzmann thrombasthenia is deficiency/dysfunction of platelet GPIIb/IIIa (integrin αIIbβ3). Platelets can adhere but cannot bind fibrinogen effectively to bridge adjacent activated platelets, so aggregation and primary platelet-plug formation are impaired.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Re-establishment of blood flow why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
B. Dissolution of platelet plug why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. Platelet plug formation correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Platelet synthesis why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.

2022 Parallel · Question 83
Confirmed
The following factors are synthesized by an intact endothelial cell except?
AI
Answer: D — Thromboxane A2
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

Healthy endothelium produces prostacyclin and nitric oxide to inhibit platelet activation and vasodilate, and stores/releases vWF. Thromboxane A2 is produced primarily by activated platelets and promotes vasoconstriction and aggregation, so it is the exception.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. vWF why not / analysis
vWF is synthesized by endothelium/megakaryocytes and stored in endothelial Weibel–Palade bodies.
B. Prostacyclin why not / analysis
This is an antiplatelet/vasodilator product of intact endothelium.
C. NO why not / analysis
This is an antiplatelet/vasodilator product of intact endothelium.
D. Thromboxane A2 correct
Correct. TXA2 is mainly generated by activated platelets and promotes vasoconstriction/aggregation; it is not an antithrombotic product of intact endothelium.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

What acts on fibrinogen to produce fibrin monomers plus fibrinopeptides A and B?
AI
Answer: A — Thrombin
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Thrombin cleaves fibrinopeptides A and B from soluble fibrinogen, creating fibrin monomers that polymerize. Factor XIIIa subsequently cross-links fibrin, making the clot mechanically stable.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Thrombin correct
Correct. Thrombin is the active protease that converts fibrinogen to fibrin and amplifies coagulation.
B. Prothrombin why not / analysis
Prothrombin is the inactive precursor (factor II); activated thrombin is the enzyme that cleaves fibrinogen.
C. Factor XIII why not / analysis
Factor XIIIa cross-links fibrin after fibrin monomers have already formed; it does not cleave fibrinogen.
D. Factor III why not / analysis
Tissue factor initiates the extrinsic pathway with factor VII; it does not directly convert fibrinogen to fibrin.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 85
Confirmed
Factor VII is activated by
AI
Answer: A — III
Confirmed · Primary haemostasis, coagulation & endothelial control

1. Start with the normal physiology

Haemostasis begins with vasoconstriction and platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial vWF via GPIb. Platelet activation releases mediators and exposes GPIIb/IIIa, allowing fibrinogen-dependent aggregation to form the primary plug. The coagulation cascade then generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin; factor XIII cross-links fibrin and stabilizes the plug. Intact endothelium normally restrains thrombosis with nitric oxide, prostacyclin, thrombomodulin/protein C and fibrinolytic activity, while storing vWF in Weibel–Palade bodies.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Platelet disorders typically cause mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae and prolonged bleeding-time/platelet-function abnormalities; coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeding and haemarthroses with characteristic PT/aPTT patterns. Glanzmann thrombasthenia is defective GPIIb/IIIa-mediated aggregation. Haemophilia A/B are factor VIII/IX deficiencies affecting the intrinsic pathway, giving prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal platelet count.

Vessel injury → vWF–GPIb adhesion → platelet activation → GPIIb/IIIa–fibrinogen aggregation → thrombin generation → fibrin stabilization

3. Why this answer follows

Tissue factor exposed after vascular injury binds factor VII/VIIa, forming the extrinsic tenase complex that activates factors X and IX. Tissue factor is therefore the physiologic trigger that functionally activates the factor-VII pathway.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. III correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
B. XIII why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
C. VII why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.
D. IX why not / analysis
This belongs to a different step of haemostasis, pathway, or laboratory test and does not match the defect specified in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 First decide: primary haemostasis (platelets/vWF) or secondary haemostasis (coagulation factors). Clinical bleeding pattern often answers this before the lab test.
Source basis: Pathology Secrets / haemostasis principles. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 86
Confirmed
The commonest variant of Hodgkin’s lymphoma?
AI
Answer: C — Nodular sclerosing
Confirmed · Hodgkin lymphoma, EBV risk & classic clinical associations

1. Start with the normal physiology

Germinal-center B cells that fail appropriate selection normally undergo apoptosis. Immune-cell interactions within lymph nodes are tightly regulated by cytokine networks.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Hodgkin lymphoma contains a minority of neoplastic Reed–Sternberg cells within a prominent reactive inflammatory background. In a subset, EBV contributes to oncogenic signalling and survival; prior infectious mononucleosis therefore increases later Hodgkin-lymphoma risk. Clinical disease often presents with painless lymphadenopathy and constitutional ‘B symptoms’; Pel–Ebstein fever is a classic historical association.

Susceptible B cell ± EBV-driven survival signalling → Reed–Sternberg clone → cytokine-rich inflammatory microenvironment → lymphadenopathy/B symptoms

3. Why this answer follows

Nodular sclerosis is the most common Hodgkin lymphoma subtype in Prof Der’s classification slide. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Lymphocyte depleted why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
B. Lymphocyte predominant why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.
C. Nodular sclerosing correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Mixed cellularity why not / analysis
This does not fit the characteristic epidemiology, histology, molecular driver or diagnostic method of the lymphoma entity in the stem.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 EBV/infectious mononucleosis is an association, not a statement that every Hodgkin lymphoma is EBV-positive.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 356–360. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 87
Confirmed
The clinical features of extravascular haemolysis include the following except?
AI
Answer: D — Haemoglobinaemia
Confirmed · Haemolysis, splenic physiology & red-cell membrane disease

1. Start with the normal physiology

RBCs must repeatedly deform through narrow splenic cords and sinusoids. Normal membrane–cytoskeleton architecture preserves the biconcave shape, surface-area-to-volume ratio and flexibility needed for this passage. Senescent or abnormal cells are removed mainly by splenic and hepatic macrophages, while haem is converted to bilirubin and iron is recycled.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Extravascular haemolysis occurs when RBCs become poorly deformable or antibody-coated and are trapped and phagocytosed in spleen/liver/marrow. The result is anaemia, reticulocytosis, unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, jaundice and often splenomegaly. In hereditary spherocytosis, defects in vertical interactions between membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer cause progressive membrane loss; cells become spherical, lose deformability and are selectively destroyed in the spleen. Chronic bilirubin overproduction predisposes to pigment gallstones.

Membrane/shape abnormality → ↓deformability → splenic trapping → macrophage phagocytosis → extravascular haemolysis → anaemia + jaundice + splenomegaly + pigment stones

3. Why this answer follows

Haemoglobinaemia is a feature of intravascular haemolysis, unlike jaundice and splenomegaly seen in extravascular destruction. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Jaundice why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
B. Splenomegaly why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
C. Hepatomegaly why not / analysis
This option represents a different red-cell defect or consequence and does not follow the mechanism targeted by the stem.
D. Haemoglobinaemia correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Distinguish site of destruction from cause. “Spleen” is not merely enlarged—it is the mechanical filter that exposes poorly deformable cells.
Source basis: Block 3.7, haemolytic anaemia section. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2022 Parallel · Question 88
Ambiguous item
The following cross-matching are true except?
AI
Answer: Ambiguous — No unambiguous “except” is identifiable from the wording.
Ambiguous item · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

A bleeding time of 4 min can be normal; 8 min can be prolonged depending on method; epinephrine is a platelet aggregation agonist; ristocetin tests vWF–GPIb-dependent platelet agglutination. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Normal bleeding time---4 minutes why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
B. Abnormal bleeding time—8 minutes why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
C. Platelet aggregation agent --- epinephrine why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
D. Ristocetin ---evaluate platelet function why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

A bleeding time of 4 min can be normal; 8 min can be prolonged depending on method; epinephrine is a platelet aggregation agonist; ristocetin tests vWF–GPIb-dependent platelet agglutination.

Source basis: Pathology Secrets / platelet-function principles. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2020

41 questions
You
2020 · Question 1
Confirmed
Intestinal causes of Vit B12 deficiency include the following except
AI
Answer: C — Pernicious anemia
Confirmed · Vitamin B12 physiology & megaloblastic pathology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Food-bound cobalamin is released in the stomach by acid and pepsin, initially binds salivary/gastric R-binders (cobalophilins/haptocorrin), is freed by pancreatic proteases in the duodenum, then binds intrinsic factor from gastric parietal cells. The intrinsic-factor–B12 complex is absorbed in the terminal ileum and B12 circulates bound mainly to transcobalamin II.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

B12 deficiency impairs methionine synthase activity and traps folate as methyl-THF, producing functionally inadequate tetrahydrofolate for DNA synthesis. Rapidly dividing tissues develop nuclear–cytoplasmic asynchrony: cytoplasm matures while nuclear DNA replication lags. This causes megaloblastic marrow change, macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils and ineffective intramedullary haematopoiesis. B12 deficiency also causes neurologic injury through disordered myelin metabolism—an important distinction from isolated folate deficiency.

Impaired B12 availability → defective DNA synthesis + abnormal myelin metabolism → megaloblastosis/ineffective erythropoiesis + neurologic disease

3. Why this answer follows

B12 absorption specifically requires an intact terminal ileum and normal handling of the B12–intrinsic-factor complex. Ileal disease/resection and competition such as bacterial overgrowth can cause deficiency. Gastric intrinsic-factor deficiency is an important cause of B12 deficiency but is not an ‘intestinal’ cause.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Ileal resection why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. Crohn's syndrome why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. Pernicious anemia correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Fish tapeworm why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
E. Intestinal loop why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Follow B12 through the GI tract step by step. A question may target the first binder, intrinsic factor source, terminal ileal absorption, pernicious anaemia or neurologic consequences.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2020 · Question 2
Confirmed
Vitamin B12 is absorbed in:
AI
Answer: B — ileum
Confirmed · Vitamin B12 physiology & megaloblastic pathology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Food-bound cobalamin is released in the stomach by acid and pepsin, initially binds salivary/gastric R-binders (cobalophilins/haptocorrin), is freed by pancreatic proteases in the duodenum, then binds intrinsic factor from gastric parietal cells. The intrinsic-factor–B12 complex is absorbed in the terminal ileum and B12 circulates bound mainly to transcobalamin II.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

B12 deficiency impairs methionine synthase activity and traps folate as methyl-THF, producing functionally inadequate tetrahydrofolate for DNA synthesis. Rapidly dividing tissues develop nuclear–cytoplasmic asynchrony: cytoplasm matures while nuclear DNA replication lags. This causes megaloblastic marrow change, macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils and ineffective intramedullary haematopoiesis. B12 deficiency also causes neurologic injury through disordered myelin metabolism—an important distinction from isolated folate deficiency.

Impaired B12 availability → defective DNA synthesis + abnormal myelin metabolism → megaloblastosis/ineffective erythropoiesis + neurologic disease

3. Why this answer follows

The intrinsic-factor–B12 complex binds specific receptors in the terminal ileum and is internalized. Therefore the terminal ileum is the key absorption site; gastric and pancreatic steps prepare the vitamin for this final uptake.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. jejunum why not / analysis
The jejunum is important for folate absorption and can absorb some iron, but the duodenum is the principal/maximal iron-absorption site.
B. ileum correct
Correct. The terminal ileum is the key site for intrinsic-factor–B12 absorption, not the main site of iron absorption.
C. duodenum why not / analysis
The proximal duodenum is the principal site of iron absorption.
D. stomach why not / analysis
The stomach prepares nutrients and secretes intrinsic factor, but it is not the principal site of iron or B12 absorption asked here.
E. colon why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Follow B12 through the GI tract step by step. A question may target the first binder, intrinsic factor source, terminal ileal absorption, pernicious anaemia or neurologic consequences.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 81–84. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2020 · Question 3
Confirmed
The first organ of haematopoiesis in a foetus is
AI
Answer: C — Yolk sac
Confirmed · Haematopoiesis & marrow physiology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal blood-cell production begins with a rare self-renewing CD34-positive pluripotent haematopoietic stem cell in the marrow. Stem cells balance self-renewal with commitment into myeloid and lymphoid progenitors. The marrow stromal microenvironment, adhesion molecules, cytokines and lineage-specific growth factors regulate survival, proliferation and differentiation. In fetal life the dominant sites shift from yolk sac to liver/spleen and finally bone marrow; in adults active marrow is concentrated in the axial skeleton and proximal long bones.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Disease appears when one of four things goes wrong: the stem/progenitor compartment is quantitatively lost, maturation is blocked, proliferation becomes autonomous, or mature cells are destroyed faster than the marrow can replace them. The peripheral blood count is therefore the end result of marrow production, release, distribution, sequestration and destruction—not simply a measure of how many cells the marrow is making.

Stem cell → committed progenitor → growth-factor signalling → maturation → marrow release → peripheral survival/distribution

3. Why this answer follows

Primitive haematopoiesis begins in blood islands of the yolk sac. As embryonic development proceeds, the liver becomes the major site, the spleen contributes, and bone marrow progressively becomes dominant. The question is therefore about the earliest site, not the eventual principal fetal site.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Fish why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. Thymus why not / analysis
The thymus is a primary lymphoid organ where T cells mature and undergo selection.
C. Yolk sac correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
D. Kidney why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.
E. Skull why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Prof. Der often asks the sequence, site, growth factor or developmental stage rather than naming the process directly. Always locate the question on this production pathway.
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 3–17. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2020 · Question 4
Confirmed
In extravascular haemaolysis, the destruction of red blood cells takes place in the following organs except:
AI
Answer: D — Heart
Confirmed · Haemolysis, splenic physiology & red-cell membrane disease

1. Start with the normal physiology

RBCs must repeatedly deform through narrow splenic cords and sinusoids. Normal membrane–cytoskeleton architecture preserves the biconcave shape, surface-area-to-volume ratio and flexibility needed for this passage. Senescent or abnormal cells are removed mainly by splenic and hepatic macrophages, while haem is converted to bilirubin and iron is recycled.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Extravascular haemolysis occurs when RBCs become poorly deformable or antibody-coated and are trapped and phagocytosed in spleen/liver/marrow. The result is anaemia, reticulocytosis, unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, jaundice and often splenomegaly. In hereditary spherocytosis, defects in vertical interactions between membrane skeleton and lipid bilayer cause progressive membrane loss; cells become spherical, lose deformability and are selectively destroyed in the spleen. Chronic bilirubin overproduction predisposes to pigment gallstones.

Membrane/shape abnormality → ↓deformability → splenic trapping → macrophage phagocytosis → extravascular haemolysis → anaemia + jaundice + splenomegaly + pigment stones

3. Why this answer follows

Extravascular haemolysis means macrophage-mediated destruction outside the vascular lumen, especially in spleen, liver and marrow where mononuclear phagocytes are abundant. The heart is not a principal reticuloendothelial site of RBC destruction.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Bone marrow why not / analysis
Bone itself is not the classic target; bone marrow is affected because haematopoietic precursors divide rapidly.
B. Spleen why not / analysis
This is a secondary lymphoid organ/tissue where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and mount immune responses.
C. Liver why not / analysis
These are not the principal tissues highlighted by B12-related megaloblastic injury in this question.
D. Heart correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.
E. Lymph nodes why not / analysis
This is a secondary lymphoid organ/tissue where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and mount immune responses.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Distinguish site of destruction from cause. “Spleen” is not merely enlarged—it is the mechanical filter that exposes poorly deformable cells.
Source basis: Block 3.7, haemolytic anaemia section. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2020 · Question 5
Confirmed
Which one of the acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is also known as the burkitts type?
AI
Answer: J — ALL3
Confirmed · Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL) & lymphoblast biology

1. Start with the normal physiology

B- and T-lymphoid precursors undergo tightly controlled antigen-receptor gene rearrangement and maturation. Immature precursors express markers such as TdT, while mature lymphocytes acquire lineage-specific surface proteins.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

ALL is a clonal malignancy of lymphoid precursors. Lymphoblast accumulation causes marrow failure and may infiltrate lymph nodes, spleen, liver, CNS or testes. B-ALL is the most common childhood acute leukaemia and typically peaks in early childhood; T-ALL often presents in adolescent males with an anterior mediastinal/thymic mass. Older FAB terminology divided ALL into L1, L2 and L3, with L3 resembling Burkitt morphology.

Lymphoid precursor mutation → maturation arrest + clonal expansion → marrow replacement/infiltration → cytopenias + organ-specific manifestations

3. Why this answer follows

FAB L3 blasts have deeply basophilic cytoplasm with cytoplasmic vacuoles and correspond historically to Burkitt-type leukaemia/lymphoma. Modern classification treats Burkitt lymphoma/leukaemia as a mature B-cell neoplasm rather than ordinary precursor ALL.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

F. AML3 why not / analysis
FAB M3 is acute promyelocytic leukaemia, classically associated with t(15;17) and DIC.
G. AML2 why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
H. ALL1 why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
I. ALL2 why not / analysis
This does not match the lineage, cytogenetic driver, maturation stage or classic complication being tested in this leukaemia question.
J. ALL3 correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use age, mediastinal mass, lineage markers and morphology together. FAB L3 is the historical “Burkitt type.”
Source basis: Block 3.7, pp. 282–296. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.
You
2020 · Question 6
Confirmed
The following can affect erythropooietin activity?
AI
Answer: J — All the above
Confirmed · Mechanism-first reasoning

1. Start with the normal physiology

The safest way to solve pathology MCQs is to identify the normal biological process being tested before naming the disease or memorized association.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Then ask where the process is disrupted: production, maturation, transport, receptor/signalling, destruction, sequestration, clonal proliferation or immune injury. The clinical and laboratory findings should follow from that causal step.

Normal process → lesion/defect → altered function → measurable finding → clinical consequence → answer

3. Why this answer follows

All listed states reduce tissue oxygen delivery or increase hypoxic drive and therefore stimulate erythropoietin. Mechanistically, trace the finding backward to the normal process: the correct option is the one that best fits the causal physiology and the pattern of tissue, marrow, blood or immune dysfunction described in the stem.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

F. hypoxaemia why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
G. left shifted-oxygen dissociation curve why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
H. severe anaemia why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
I. Altitude why not / analysis
This option is less appropriate because it does not best match the mechanism, definition or association tested in the stem.
J. All the above correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Use the mechanism to reject distractors, especially when the wording is imperfect.
Source basis: Block 3.7 slides; Pathology Secrets used as fallback where needed. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2020 · Question 7
Multiple correct
The following are true about blood group O
AI
Answer: Multiple — Multiple statements are true: group O red cells are used as universal/emergency donor red cells (especially O-negative), and appropriately selected group O blood may be used in neonatal transfusion.
Multiple correct · ABO/Rh immunohaematology & transfusion compatibility

1. Start with the normal physiology

ABO antigens are carbohydrate structures on RBC membranes. Individuals naturally develop mainly IgM antibodies against the ABO antigen(s) they lack. Group A has A antigen and anti-B; group B has B antigen and anti-A; group AB has both antigens and neither anti-A nor anti-B; group O has neither A nor B antigen and has both antibodies in plasma. A and B alleles are codominant; O is recessive.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

If incompatible donor RBC antigens encounter pre-existing recipient antibodies, antibody binding activates complement and causes potentially catastrophic intravascular haemolysis. Pretransfusion testing therefore identifies ABO/Rh type, screens for unexpected antibodies and crossmatches recipient plasma against donor RBCs. Forward grouping tests patient RBCs with known antisera; reverse grouping tests patient plasma against known reagent cells.

Donor RBC antigen + recipient antibody → agglutination/complement → intravascular haemolysis → shock/DIC/renal injury

3. Why this answer follows

Group O RBCs lack A and B antigens; group O plasma contains both anti-A and anti-B. ‘Universal donor’ applies mainly to packed O RBCs—especially O-negative in emergencies—not to unrestricted transfusion of whole group O blood, because donor plasma antibodies can matter. Any statement that puts anti-A/anti-B ‘on the RBC’ confuses antigen with antibody.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

D. first blood in human why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
E. Less common in Africa why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.
F. can be given to a neonate why not / analysis
Because this item is malformed, incomplete or has more than one defensible statement, this option must be judged against the detailed note rather than treated as automatically wrong.

5. Prof. Der exam angle

💡 Keep “antigen on RBC” separate from “antibody in plasma.” Many badly worded past questions deliberately blur these compartments.

6. Question-quality note

The option saying group O RBCs “lack anti-A and anti-B antibodies” is badly worded: RBCs lack A/B antigens; anti-A and anti-B are in plasma.

Source basis: Transfusion-medicine principle. The Masterpiece PDF was used for question stems, not as an answer authority.

2020 · Question 8
Confirmed
Bones involved in hemopoiesis EXCEPT……….
AI
Answer: E — Digits
Confirmed · Haematopoiesis & marrow physiology

1. Start with the normal physiology

Normal blood-cell production begins with a rare self-renewing CD34-positive pluripotent haematopoietic stem cell in the marrow. Stem cells balance self-renewal with commitment into myeloid and lymphoid progenitors. The marrow stromal microenvironment, adhesion molecules, cytokines and lineage-specific growth factors regulate survival, proliferation and differentiation. In fetal life the dominant sites shift from yolk sac to liver/spleen and finally bone marrow; in adults active marrow is concentrated in the axial skeleton and proximal long bones.

2. What changes in disease / pathophysiology

Disease appears when one of four things goes wrong: the stem/progenitor compartment is quantitatively lost, maturation is blocked, proliferation becomes autonomous, or mature cells are destroyed faster than the marrow can replace them. The peripheral blood count is therefore the end result of marrow production, release, distribution, sequestration and destruction—not simply a measure of how many cells the marrow is making.

Stem cell → committed progenitor → growth-factor signalling → maturation → marrow release → peripheral survival/distribution

3. Why this answer follows

At birth nearly all marrow is haematopoietically active. With age, much of the distal appendicular marrow becomes fatty. Adult active marrow is concentrated in vertebrae, ribs, sternum, skull, pelvis and proximal humerus/femur—not the digits.

4. Every option — why correct or why wrong

A. Skull why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
B. Femur why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
C. Humerus why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
D. Lumbar vertebrae why not / analysis
This option belongs to a different nutrient, organ, developmental stage, regulatory signal or red-cell parameter than the one being tested.
E. Digits correct
Correct. This option best fits the physiology/pathophysiology and the source hierarchy used for this key.

💡 Prof. Der often asks the sequence, site, growth factor or developmental stage rather than naming the process directly. Always locate the question on this production pathway.
